author: Copilot
body: The Email validation regex does not support international (Unicode) email addresses, which are valid according to modern email standards. The regex `^[A-Za-z0-9+_.-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.-]+$` will reject valid emails with non-ASCII characters like `test@世界.com`. Consider using a more permissive regex or a proper email validation library if you need to support international domain names and local parts.

author: Copilot
body: This PR adds a new public `DerivationBuilder.instance(typeId, termName, instance)` overload, but repository docs currently describe only two `instance` overloads on `DerivationBuilder` (see `docs/reference/type-class-derivation.md`). Please update the docs to include this new term-name override API and clarify that `typeId` refers to the *parent* record/variant type (and that the override is unchecked w.r.t. the actual term type).

author: Copilot
body:
This adds a new public `DerivationBuilder.modifier(typeId, termName, modifier)` overload, but docs currently list only two `modifier` overloads. Please update the relevant docs section (e.g. `docs/reference/type-class-derivation.md`) to document this API and its semantics (term name inside a parent type identified by `typeId`).
```suggestion

  /** Adds a term-level modifier for a member identified by `termName` inside a parent type
    * identified by the given `typeId`.
    *
    * This overload is useful when you want to target a specific field or case by name within a
    * type, without having an `Optic` for that member. The modifier will be applied when deriving
    * instances for any schema path where the given `typeId` and `termName` pair matches.
    *
    * @param typeId
    *   the `TypeId` of the parent type that owns the term to be modified
    * @param termName
    *   the name of the term (field or case) within the parent type to which the modifier applies
    * @param modifier
    *   the term-level modifier to apply to the targeted member
    * @tparam B
    *   the parent type identified by `typeId`
    * @return
    *   a new [[DerivationBuilder]] including this term-level modifier override
    */
```

author: Copilot
body: New public overload `instance(typeId, termName, instance)` was added without Scaladoc. Since this is part of the public derivation API and the semantics are non-obvious (the `typeId` is the parent node type, and the instance type is not statically tied to the term), please add a Scaladoc comment describing usage and caveats.

author: Copilot
body:
New public overload `modifier(typeId, termName, modifier)` was added without Scaladoc. Please document what the `typeId` refers to (the parent node type) and how the term name is matched (record field / variant case name).
```suggestion

  /** Registers a term-level modifier for a specific field or variant case of a parent node type.
    *
    * The `typeId` identifies the parent node type that owns the term to be modified (for example,
    * the record type that declares the field, or the enum/variant type that declares the case).
    * The `termName` is matched by name against either a record field name or a variant case name
    * within that parent type.
    *
    * @tparam B       the parent node type whose term is being modified
    * @param typeId   the `TypeId` of the parent node type that contains the target field or case
    * @param termName the name of the record field or variant case to which the modifier is applied
    * @param modifier the term-level modifier to register for the specified term
    * @return         a new [[DerivationBuilder]] including the additional modifier override
    *
    * @example
    * {{{
    * val builder =
    *   Schema.derive[Person].deriving[Eq]
    *     .modifier(PersonTypeId, "age", Modifier.rename("years"))
    * }}}
    */
```

author: Copilot
body:
In `replaceFieldInstance`, the `Reflect.Deferred` branch constructs a new `Deferred` using only `_value` and `_typeId`, which drops any deferred default value / examples stored in the original `Deferred`. This changes schema metadata behavior for deferred nodes when applying term-name overrides.

Consider using `copy(_value = ...)` (or otherwise preserving the additional constructor params) so defaults/examples are retained.
```suggestion
          d.copy(_value = () => replaceFieldInstance(d._value(), newInstance))
```

body:
The type cast uses incorrect type parameter. The binding has type `Binding[BindingType.Seq[C], C[A]]`, so when casting to `BindingInstance[TC, ?, X]`, the third type parameter `X` should be `C[A]` (the collection type), not `A` (the element type). Consider using `BindingInstance[TC, ?, ?]` with a wildcard for the third parameter, as done in `deriveMap` and `deriveDynamic`, or use the correct type `C[A]`.
```suggestion
            } else binding.asInstanceOf[BindingInstance[TC, ?, ?]].instance.force
```

author: Copilot
body:
The codec derivation for record fields should use `D.instance(field.value.metadata).force` for consistency with how codecs are derived in `deriveSequence`, `deriveMap`, and `deriveWrapper`. The current approach of directly accessing `.asInstanceOf[BindingInstance[TC, ?, A]].instance` uses incorrect type parameters (where `A` is the record type rather than the field type) and is inconsistent with the rest of the codebase. While this works due to type erasure, it makes the code harder to understand and maintain.
```suggestion
                  val codec =
                    D.instance(field.value.metadata).force
                      .asInstanceOf[AvroBinaryCodec[?]]
```

author: Copilot
body:
The codec derivation for variant cases should use `D.instance(cases(idx).value.metadata).force` for consistency with how codecs are derived in `deriveSequence`, `deriveMap`, and `deriveWrapper`. The current approach of directly accessing `.asInstanceOf[BindingInstance[TC, ?, A]].instance` is inconsistent with the pattern used elsewhere in this file.
```suggestion
                codecs(idx) =
                  D.instance(cases(idx).value.metadata)
                    .force
                    .asInstanceOf[AvroBinaryCodec[A]]
```

author: Copilot
body:
This public API declaration is missing required Scaladoc documentation. According to project guidelines, all new public APIs must have comprehensive Scaladoc including:

- A clear one-line summary explaining what this given instance does
- `@tparam` tags for type parameters `A` and `C` explaining their purpose
- A usage example demonstrating how to use this integration with Iron constraints
- `@return` tag (if applicable for given instances)

The Scaladoc should explain that this automatically derives a Schema for Iron-refined types that validates the constraint during decoding and unwraps during encoding.

Example structure:
```
/** Automatically derives a Schema for Iron-refined types.
  *
  * This given instance enables automatic schema derivation for types refined with Iron constraints.
  * During decoding, the constraint is validated and failure results in a SchemaError. During
  * encoding, the refined value is unwrapped to its base type.
  *
  * @tparam A the base type being refined
  * @tparam C the Iron constraint being applied
  * @example
  * {{{
  * import io.github.iltotore.iron.*
  * import io.github.iltotore.iron.constraint.numeric.*
  * import zio.blocks.schema.*
  * import zio.blocks.schema.iron.given
  *
  * case class Person(age: Int :| Positive)
  * given Schema[Person] = Schema.derived
  * }}}
  */
```

author: Copilot
body:
These tests are inadequate and don't actually verify the core functionality of the Iron integration. The tests only check that schemas can be summoned (schema != null), but don't test:

1. **Validation on decode** - The primary feature: that invalid values are rejected with appropriate error messages
2. **Valid values pass** - That valid refined values decode successfully
3. **Round-trip behavior** - That encoding and decoding work correctly
4. **Error messages** - That validation failures produce clear, helpful error messages

The PR description claims "100% coverage" and states that "Decoding with validation works correctly", but these tests don't exercise any validation logic at all.

Recommended tests to add:
- Test decoding valid refined values (e.g., positive integers) succeeds
- Test decoding invalid values (e.g., negative integers for Positive constraint) fails with appropriate error
- Test round-trip encoding/decoding of valid refined values
- Test that error messages include the constraint violation details

You can use patterns from `JsonTestUtils.roundTrip` and `JsonTestUtils.decodeError` that are available via the test dependency on schema's test sources.

author: Copilot
body: `dynamicValueToReflect` now uses `getOrDefault` for fields like `typeId`, `fields`, `element`, etc. This means malformed/partial schema encodings can silently produce `Reflect.Dynamic` / `TypeId.Unknown` / empty field lists instead of failing fast. If this behavior change is intended, it should be made explicit (e.g., by returning a `SchemaError`/throwing `SchemaError.conversionFailed` when required fields are absent, or by clearly documenting that decoding is best-effort and falls back to `Reflect.Dynamic`).

author: Copilot
body: There doesn’t appear to be a regression test covering the new “best-effort” decoding path (e.g., missing `typeId` / `fields` / `element` keys) to ensure `DynamicSchema.fromDynamicValue` doesn’t throw and produces the expected fallback `Reflect` structure. Adding a focused test would lock in the intended safety behavior and prevent accidental reintroduction of exceptions.

author: Copilot
body:
`new java.util.HashMap[...] { ... }` creates an anonymous subclass just to run side-effecting initialization. This adds bytecode/alloc overhead and makes the intent harder to read. Prefer constructing a plain `HashMap` and populating it in a subsequent `fields.foreach(...)` (or a while loop) to keep it allocation-free and clearer.
```suggestion
        val fieldMap = new java.util.HashMap[String, DynamicValue](fields.length)
        fields.foreach(kv => fieldMap.put(kv._1, kv._2))
```

author: Copilot
body:
The default for missing `"value"` is `emptyDynamicRecord`, while other missing reflect sub-fields default to `DynamicValue.Null`. Using a Record here is semantically confusing (it’s not a reflect encoding) and may hide malformed input. Consider defaulting to `DynamicValue.Null` (or explicitly branching on `get("value") == null`) for consistency and clearer intent.
```suggestion
        val valueDv   = fieldMap.get("value") match {
          case null  => DynamicValue.Null
          case other => other
        }
        val value     = dynamicValueToReflect(valueDv)
```

author: Copilot
body: `foldDownOrFail` is supposed to traverse top-down (apply `f` to parents before children), but this branch delegates to `foldUpOrFail` for children. That changes traversal order and can change results for order-dependent folds. Use `foldDownOrFail` recursively here (and in the other container branches) instead of `foldUpOrFail`.

author: Copilot
body:
Same issue in the Sequence branch: `foldDownOrFail` calls `foldUpOrFail` on child elements, which makes the traversal bottom-up for descendants. This should call `foldDownOrFail` so `f` is applied before descending.
```suggestion
              foldDownOrFail(e, path.at(idx), b, f) match {
```

author: Copilot
body: Same issue in the Map branch: `foldDownOrFail` uses `foldUpOrFail` for both key and value traversal, which violates the documented top-down semantics. Switch these recursive calls to `foldDownOrFail` to preserve pre-order traversal and proper short-circuiting behavior.

author: Copilot
body:
In the Variant case, `foldDownOrFail` delegates to `foldUpOrFail`, which again changes traversal order. This should call `foldDownOrFail(v.value, ...)` so the fold remains top-down throughout the structure.
```suggestion
          case v: Variant => foldDownOrFail(v.value, path.caseOf(v.caseNameValue), z, f)
```

author: Copilot
body: `mergeByKey` no longer deduplicates keys the way the previous `toMap`-based implementation effectively did. Because `leftMap` is only populated from `leftFields` (and not updated when appending right-only keys), duplicate keys in `rightFields` will now produce multiple entries in the merged object (with potentially different values), whereas previously duplicates would all resolve to the last right value. Similarly, duplicate keys in `leftFields` are now preserved and only the last occurrence is merged. If duplicate keys are possible (they are in `jsonCodec` decoding), consider tracking/merging by the first/last seen key consistently (e.g., update the index map when adding right-only keys, and decide whether earlier duplicates should be overwritten or merged) so `merge` behavior is deterministic and matches the documented “union of keys” semantics.

author: Copilot
body: There are merge tests in `JsonSpec`, but none cover objects with duplicate keys (which can be produced by `Json.parse` because the codec doesn’t reject duplicates). Adding a regression test for `merge` on objects containing duplicate keys (on both left and right) would help lock in the intended semantics and prevent accidental behavior changes in future optimizations.

body: `Json.parse(Chunk[Byte])` now pattern-matches on the concrete `Chunk.ByteArray` implementation and relies on `offset`/`array` internals. This couples schema/json parsing to Chunk’s internal representation and drove the visibility widening in `Chunk.scala`; consider adding a stable public API on `Chunk` (e.g., an accessor returning an `(Array[Byte], from, to)` slice) to avoid leaking/internal-type dependencies.

author: Copilot
body: Changing these array-backed `Chunk` implementations from `private` to `private[blocks]` (and exposing `offset`) expands the library’s effective API surface and makes internal representations accessible to downstream code (anyone can put code in the `zio.blocks` package). If the goal is just zero-copy JSON parsing, consider a narrower, dedicated API (or extractor) rather than widening visibility for all array chunk types.

author: Copilot
body:
`sdlTypeRepr` no longer special-cases `TypeRepr.ParamRef`, so printing type arguments that are type parameters will fall back to the case class `toString` (e.g. `ParamRef(TypeParam(...), ...)`) instead of the parameter name. This looks like a regression from the previous implementation and makes SDL output noisy for higher-kinded / polymorphic type ids. Consider restoring a `ParamRef` case that renders `param.name` (and any other cases that previously required custom rendering).
```suggestion
    case TypeRepr.ParamRef(param, _) => param.name
    case r: TypeRepr.Ref             => sdlTypeName(r.id.asInstanceOf[TypeId[Any]])
    case _                           => repr.toString
```

author: Copilot
body:
The variable `isFirst` is declared inside the `foreach` lambda but is intended to track state across iterations. In Scala's foreach, the lambda is invoked for each element, and the `var isFirst = true` declaration will be re-executed for every iteration, making it always `true`. This means `sb.append("\n")` will never be called, breaking the multi-line formatting.

To fix this, declare `var isFirst = true` before the `foreach` call, outside the lambda body.

body:
`ScopedResourceOps` is a new public API, but its Scaladoc is incomplete compared to `ScopedOps`: the implicit class doc is missing an `@param sr` tag for the constructor parameter, and `def allocate` lacks its own Scaladoc (including a `@return` description). Please align this with the existing style used for `get` so the new API is fully documented.
```suggestion
   *
   * @param sr
   *   the scoped `Resource` description to allocate within this scope
   * @tparam A
   *   the underlying resource value type
   */
  implicit class ScopedResourceOps[A](private val sr: $[Resource[A]]) {

    /**
     * Allocates the resource described by a scoped `Resource[A]`.
     *
     * This acquires the resource within this scope and returns its result value
     * as a scoped `$[A]`, without ever exposing the underlying `Resource`.
     *
     * @return
     *   a scoped value containing the allocated resource result, or a
     *   default-valued scoped value if this scope is already closed
     */
```

author: Copilot
body:
The test suite lacks coverage for match/case expressions where the parameter appears in the scrutinee position. While the macro implementation handles this case (lines 181-186 in UseMacros.scala for Scala 3, lines 194-199 in ScopeMacros.scala for Scala 2), there should be a test verifying that patterns like `use(db)(d => d match { case x => ... })` are properly rejected.

Consider adding a test case:
```scala
test("match expression with param as scrutinee is rejected") {
  assertZIO(typeCheck("""
    Scope.global.scoped { scope =>
      import scope._
      val db: $[Database] = allocate(Resource.from[Database])
      scope.use(db)(d => d match { case x => "matched" })
    }
  """))(isLeft)
}
```

author: Copilot
body:
The use of `leak` here undermines the compile-time safety guarantees introduced by this PR. The purpose of the macro-enforced `use` is to prevent resource leaks, but calling `leak` to extract `DbConnection` from `$[DbConnection]` reintroduces the leak risk.

Consider refactoring `beginTransaction` to accept `$[DbConnection]` and use `use` internally, or make it a method that works within the scope pattern. For example:

```scala
// Option 1: Accept scoped connection
def withTransaction[A](conn: $[DbConnection])(f: $[DbTransaction] => A): A

// Option 2: Make it work with use
scope.use(conn)(c => scope.allocate(c.beginTransaction("tx-001")))
```

The current pattern with `leak` defeats the safety model this PR introduces.

author: Copilot
body:
Using `leak` here undermines the compile-time safety guarantees of this PR. The pattern `scope.leak(pool)` followed by `allocate(Resource(rawPool.lease()))` reintroduces resource leak risks that the macro-enforced `use` was designed to prevent.

Consider refactoring the `Pool.lease()` method to return `Resource[Connection]` or use a different pattern that doesn't require leaking the scoped value.

author: Copilot
body:
Using `leak` extensively in examples undermines the compile-time safety guarantees introduced by this PR. The pattern of leaking scoped values to call methods on them defeats the purpose of macro-enforced `use`.

Consider refactoring the `ConnectionPool.acquire` method to return `Resource[PooledConnection]` directly, or use a helper method that works within the scope safety model.

author: Copilot
body:
Scaladoc says `recycle` is invoked when the value is returned "while the pool is still open", but `Pool.releaseEntry` only checks `isClosed` once before calling `recycle`, so `close()` can race and `recycle` may still run even if the pool becomes closed concurrently. Please clarify the contract to mention this race (e.g., recycle runs if the pool was observed open at release time; if close wins, the entry will still be destroyed instead of reused).
```suggestion
 *   a function invoked when a leased value is returned to the pool and the
 *   pool is observed to be open at release time. Due to the inherent race
 *   between releasing a value and closing the pool, `recycle` may still run
 *   even if the pool becomes closed concurrently after this observation. In
 *   that case the recycled value will be destroyed rather than reused. The
 *   function must reset the value for the next lease. If it throws, the value
 *   is destroyed and the release fails with that exception (or with a
 *   finalization exception that suppresses it when destruction also fails).
```

body:
`appendPath` drops all `DynamicOptic.Node` types other than `Field` and `AtIndex` (the wildcard case does nothing). This makes `JsonPatch.toString` misleading for valid JSON patch paths that include nodes like `Elements` (`[*]`) or `Wrapped`, because those segments disappear from the rendered path. Consider delegating to `DynamicOptic.renderString` (as `DynamicPatch` does) or explicitly rendering the other supported node types instead of silently ignoring them.
```suggestion
        case f: DynamicOptic.Node.Field      => sb.append('.').append(f.name)
        case ai: DynamicOptic.Node.AtIndex   => sb.append('[').append(ai.index).append(']')
        case _: DynamicOptic.Node.Elements   => sb.append("[*]")
        case _: DynamicOptic.Node.Wrapped    => sb.append(".wrapped")
```

author: Copilot
body:
The usage counter uses `grep -rl "\b${tname}\b" ...` without `-P`/`-E`, so `\b` is treated as a literal backspace in basic regex and will not match word boundaries. This likely makes `usage` always 0 and breaks the “referenced in 3+ source files” prioritization. Use `grep -rlw -- "$tname" ...` (or `grep -rE "\\b${tname}\\b"`) to get correct word-boundary matches.
```suggestion
  usage=$(grep -rlw -- "$tname" "$ROOT" --include='*.scala' 2>/dev/null \
```

That makes the scan non-idempotent: once the report exists, types listed multiple times in the report (e.g. `MediaType`) can be incorrectly treated as “documented” on subsequent runs. Exclude the generated report (and any other tracking docs) from the docs corpus used for mention counting / ID discovery, or generate the report outside `docs/`.

author: Copilot
body:
The condition `[ -z "$pkg" ] || [ "$pkg" = "unknown" ] && continue` has incorrect operator precedence. The `&&` operator binds tighter than `||`, so this is evaluated as `[ -z "$pkg" ] || ([ "$pkg" = "unknown" ] && continue)`, which means the continue only happens when pkg equals "unknown", not when pkg is empty. This should be written as `([ -z "$pkg" ] || [ "$pkg" = "unknown" ]) && continue` or split into two separate conditions for clarity.
```suggestion
    [ -z "$pkg" ] && continue
    [ "$pkg" = "unknown" ] && continue
```

author: Copilot
body:
`buildStringValidation` now constructs `Validation.String.Length(minLen, maxLen)` whenever either bound is defined. This changes behavior when `minLength` is explicitly `0` (and `maxLength` is absent): previously it returned `Validation.None`, but now it returns `Length(Some(0), None)`, which can become an observable constraint (e.g., in rendered schema / validation suffixes) even though it’s semantically redundant. Consider preserving the old logic by only emitting `Length` when `min.exists(_ > 0)` or `max.isDefined`, and avoid `Option.get`/`ne None` in favor of `isDefined`/pattern matching for clarity.
```suggestion
        val hasMin = minLen.exists(_ > 0)
        val hasMax = maxLen.isDefined
        if (hasMin || hasMax) {
          (minLen, maxLen) match {
            case (Some(1), Some(1)) =>
              Validation.String.NonEmpty
            case _ =>
              new Validation.String.Length(minLen, maxLen)
          }
```

author: Copilot
body:
The table lists `encode(value, output: OutputStream)` / `decode(input: InputStream)` as common methods across binary codecs, but `MessagePackBinaryCodec` and `ThriftBinaryCodec` only support `Array[Byte]`/`ByteBuffer` (no stream overloads). Consider marking these rows as JSON/TOON/Avro-only (or removing them from the “common” list).
```suggestion
`BinaryCodec` subclasses (JSON, TOON, MessagePack, Avro, Thrift) expose the following convenience overloads (availability may vary by format):

| Method                                               | Description                                  |
|------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| `encode(value): Array[Byte]`                         | Encode to a byte array                       |
| `decode(input: Array[Byte]): Either[SchemaError, A]` | Decode from a byte array                     |
| `encode(value, output: ByteBuffer): Unit`            | Encode into a `ByteBuffer`                   |
| `decode(input: ByteBuffer): Either[SchemaError, A]`  | Decode from a `ByteBuffer`                   |
| `encode(value, output: OutputStream): Unit`          | Encode into an `OutputStream` (JSON, TOON, Avro)   |
| `decode(input: InputStream): Either[SchemaError, A]` | Decode from an `InputStream` (JSON, TOON, Avro)    |
| `encodeToString(value): String`                      | Encode to a `String` (JSON, TOON)            |
| `decode(input: String): Either[SchemaError, A]`      | Decode from a `String` (JSON, TOON)          |
```

This overview says a single `Schema[A]` can derive codecs for “BSON” via the `Codec`/`Format` system, but BSON support in this repo is provided via `BsonSchemaCodec` and does not implement `codec.Format` / `codec.Codec`. Please adjust the text to avoid implying BSON is derived via `Schema.derive(format)` or that it is a `Codec` subtype.
```suggestion
`Codec[DecodeInput, EncodeOutput, Value]` is the base abstraction for encoding and decoding values between a specific input representation and a specific output representation. It forms the foundation of ZIO Blocks' multi-format serialization system, enabling a single `Schema[A]` to derive codecs for JSON, Avro, TOON, MessagePack, Thrift, and other formats that are integrated via the `Codec`/`Format` system. BSON support is provided separately via `BsonSchemaCodec`, which is not a subtype of `codec.Codec` and is not derived via `Schema.derive(format)`.
```

body:
This sentence says all `BinaryCodec` subclasses share the same overload set, but `MessagePackBinaryCodec` and `ThriftBinaryCodec` don’t implement the stream-based overloads shown below. Please narrow the claim to the codecs that actually provide those overloads, or qualify the table entries by format.
```suggestion
`BinaryCodec` implementations (for example JSON, TOON, MessagePack, Avro, Thrift) expose the following convenience overloads; availability may vary by format as noted:

| Method                                               | Description                                      |
|------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------|
| `encode(value): Array[Byte]`                         | Encode to a byte array                           |
| `decode(input: Array[Byte]): Either[SchemaError, A]` | Decode from a byte array                         |
| `encode(value, output: ByteBuffer): Unit`            | Encode into a `ByteBuffer`                       |
| `decode(input: ByteBuffer): Either[SchemaError, A]`  | Decode from a `ByteBuffer`                       |
| `encode(value, output: OutputStream): Unit`          | Encode into an `OutputStream` (JSON, TOON, Avro; not MessagePack, Thrift) |
| `decode(input: InputStream): Either[SchemaError, A]` | Decode from an `InputStream` (JSON, TOON, Avro; not MessagePack, Thrift)  |
| `encodeToString(value): String`                      | Encode to a `String` (JSON, TOON)                |
| `decode(input: String): Either[SchemaError, A]`      | Decode from a `String` (JSON, TOON)              |
```

body:
The `Gen.char` filter doesn’t actually exclude surrogate code points: it includes `0xD800` and `0xDFFF` due to the `<= 0xd800` / `>= 0xdfff` bounds, and the boolean expression is hard to read without explicit parentheses. This can let invalid UTF-16 surrogate chars through and make the property-based test flaky. Consider changing the predicate to exclude the full surrogate range (0xD800–0xDFFF inclusive) and clarifying the grouping with parentheses.
```suggestion
        check(Gen.char.filter(x => x >= ' ' && (x < '\uD800' || x > '\uDFFF'))) { // excluding control and surrogate chars
```

body:
The string generator predicate intended to exclude surrogate chars still allows boundary surrogate values because it uses `x <= 0xd800` / `x >= 0xdfff`. To truly exclude the surrogate range, use strict bounds (`x < 0xD800 || x > 0xDFFF`) and consider adding parentheses to make the `&&`/`||` grouping unambiguous.
```suggestion
              Gen.char.filter(x => (x >= ' ' && (x < 0xd800 || x > 0xdfff)) && x != '"' && x != '\\' && x != 0xff)
```

author: Copilot
body:
The statement that "All operations are lenient by default" is inaccurate for `DynamicValue#get`: `get(path)` returns a `DynamicValueSelection` that becomes a failure when the path is not found (it doesn’t return the original value unchanged). Consider rewording to clarify that lenient behavior applies to `modify`/`set`/`delete`/`insert` (which return the original value on missing paths), while `get` reports a path-not-found error (though `.toChunk` will yield an empty chunk on failure).
```suggestion
`DynamicOptic` is the path argument for all `DynamicValue` operations: `get(path)` for retrieval (with `.one` or `.toChunk`), `modify(path)(f)` for transformation, `set(path, value)` for replacement, `delete(path)` for removal, and `insert(path, value)` for addition. By default, the mutating operations (`modify`, `set`, `delete`, `insert`) are lenient and return the original value unchanged if the path doesn't resolve, whereas `get(path)` yields a failing `DynamicValueSelection` on a missing path (though calling `.toChunk` on it will produce an empty chunk). Each operation also has a strict `*OrFail` variant returning `Either[SchemaError, DynamicValue]` with error details on failure.
```

     since = "0.1.0"

```

Since the expanded tree is typechecked in the caller’s context, `leak(...)` may not compile for users outside the `zio.blocks.scope` package due to access restrictions. Consider having the macro call a public unsafe-unwrapping method (with the warning behavior centralized in `leak`) rather than invoking a package-private member directly.

body: The docs describe `A @@ S`/`Scoped[A, S]` as a deferred computation; however, `Scope.allocate` acquires the resource immediately (it calls `resource.make(this)` eagerly) and only wraps the already-acquired value in a thunk for later access. It would help to explicitly document that `allocate` is eager in acquisition but returns a deferred handle for accessing/composing the value, to avoid implying that acquisition itself is deferred until `scope.execute`.

`uniqueCheck` uses a somewhat tricky `forall { val seen = ...; v => ... }` pattern (a block returning a lambda that captures `seen`). This is easy to misread; consider rewriting to declare `seen` first and then call `forall(v => seen.add(v))` for clarity.

    private[this] def uniqueCheck(values: Chunk[Json]): Boolean = {

author: Copilot
body: `toJsonSchema` computes the `required` set based on the *encoding* transient flags (`skipDefaultValue`/`skipNone`/`skipEmptyCollection`). This makes generated schemas overly strict when, e.g., `withTransientNone(false)` (optional fields become required) even though decode-optional semantics are controlled by `requireOptionFields` / `requireCollectionFields` / `requireDefaultValueFields` (already reflected in `FieldInfo.isOptional/isCollection/hasDefault`). Consider reverting requiredness to `!fieldInfo.isOptional && !fieldInfo.isCollection && !fieldInfo.hasDefault` (or equivalent) so schema required fields match decoding/shape semantics rather than serialization omission policy.

body: `normalizedTypeReprEqual` normalizes both sides on every recursive call (because recursive comparisons call `normalizedTypeReprEqual` again). For deeply nested container types, this can lead to repeated normalization work. Consider splitting into (1) a public `normalizedTypeReprEqual` that normalizes once at the top, and (2) an internal helper (e.g., `normalizedTypeReprEqualNormalized`) that assumes inputs are already normalized and only recurses structurally. Apply the same pattern to hashing if you see similar repeated normalization costs there.

Copilot
body: These assertions are too permissive: escaping a double quote in an attribute value should result in `"` (since attributes are written with `\"...\"`), and escaping a single quote should result in `'`. Allowing either entity can let incorrect behavior pass (e.g., replacing `\"` with `'` changes the attribute value semantics). Tighten the expectations to the specific entity that should be produced for each input.

Prefer returning one of the errors unchanged, or construct a combined error message without pretending it’s a structured path node (e.g., keep `e1` and append `e2` message into `e1`’s message, or expose a dedicated aggregation strategy).

    case l @ Left(_) =>

        case Left(_) => l

author: Copilot
body:
This example doesn’t match the actual `XmlName` API in the code: `namespace`/`prefix` are `Option[String]` (not empty strings), and `XmlName(\"atom:feed\")` does not parse the prefix/local name (it stores `localName = \"atom:feed\"` with `prefix = None`). Consider updating the doc to use `namespaceOrEmpty` / `qualifiedName` (or show `namespace = None`, `prefix = None`), and use `XmlName.parse(\"atom:feed\")` when demonstrating prefix parsing.
```suggestion
simple.namespace     // None
simple.prefix        // None

// With namespace URI
val ns = XmlName("person", "http://example.com/ns")
ns.localName         // "person"
ns.namespace         // Some("http://example.com/ns")
ns.prefix            // None

// With prefix (parsed from qualified name)
val prefixed = XmlName.parse("atom:feed")
prefixed.localName   // "feed"
prefixed.prefix      // Some("atom")
// prefixed.qualifiedName // "atom:feed"
```

author: Copilot
body: `toJsonSchema` builds `names`/`schemas` arrays sized to `fieldInfos.length` but only populates indices for `nonTransient` fields. Transient fields leave null entries that are still passed into `ChunkMap.fromChunks(Chunk.fromArray(names), ...)`, which will produce a map containing null keys/values (or later NPEs) and also incorrectly exposes transient fields in the schema. Build the properties collection using only non-transient fields (e.g., count non-transient first and allocate compact arrays, or use a builder filtered on `fi.nonTransient`).

`detectContexts` now calls `parts.length` unconditionally. For general `Seq` this can be O(n) (and for some Seq-like inputs can traverse), whereas the previous implementation avoided computing length for the empty case. Consider using a constant-time check such as `parts.isEmpty || parts.tail.isEmpty` (or `parts.iterator.take(2).length <= 1` / `sizeIs <= 1` when available) to keep this a true fast-path.
```suggestion
    if (parts.isEmpty || parts.tail.isEmpty) Right(Nil)
```

`checkPrimitive` compares `TypeId`s using reference equality (`ne`). `TypeId` implements structural `equals`, so this will reject valid primitives when the `TypeId` instances are equal-but-not-identical (e.g., reconstructed/derived IDs). Use `!=`/`==` (or `TypeId.structurallyEqual`) instead of `ne` here.
```suggestion
        if (pt.typeId != pv.primitiveType.typeId) {
```

author: Copilot
Copilot
body:
`normalizeTypeRepr` can construct “nested applied” shapes (e.g. `Applied(Applied(...), args)`) or leave un-substituted alias parameters when a type alias is used as the *tycon* of an `Applied` node (i.e. `TypeRepr.Applied(TypeRepr.Ref(aliasId), args)`). This happens because alias expansion is only handled for `TypeRepr.Ref(id)` and the alias branch uses `TypeRepr.Ref(normalize(underlying))` which can still be `isApplied`, which then gets rewritten later into `Applied(Ref(unapplied), ...)` and can become nested under the outer `Applied`.
```suggestion
      val normArgs  = args.map(normalizeTypeRepr)
      val normTycon = normalizeTypeRepr(tycon)
      normTycon match {
        case TypeRepr.Applied(innerTycon, innerArgs) =>
          // Flatten nested applied types to avoid shapes like Applied(Applied(...), args)
          TypeRepr.Applied(innerTycon, innerArgs ++ normArgs)
        case otherTycon =>
          TypeRepr.Applied(otherTycon, normArgs)
      }
```

author: Copilot
body: `normalizedTypeReprEqual` normalizes on entry, but it is also called recursively via `normalizedTypeArgsEqual` / tuple element comparison, which means nested comparisons will repeatedly re-normalize subtrees. This can add avoidable overhead for deeply nested/large `TypeRepr`s.

author: Copilot
body: The new regression suite covers direct alias vs underlying and `List[...]` cases, but it doesn’t currently cover the additional scenarios called out in the PR description (e.g., `Option[FooId]` and nested containers). Since the bug is about alias normalization inside type arguments, adding coverage for at least one other type constructor (e.g. `Option`) and a nested generic type (e.g. `List[Option[FooId]]`) would reduce the risk of regressions in the recursive normalization logic.

author: Copilot
body:
API table entry for `catchAll` doesn’t match the implementation’s type parameterization (`catchAll[B >: A](...)`). As written, `catchAll(f: Throwable => Lazy[B]): Lazy[B]` suggests you can recover to any unrelated `B`, which won’t typecheck. Please update the signature in the docs to match the actual method.
```suggestion
| `force: A`                                          | Evaluate and return the result (memoized) |
| `isEvaluated: Boolean`                              | Check if the value has been computed      |
| `map[B](f: A => B): Lazy[B]`                        | Transform the value                       |
| `flatMap[B](f: A => Lazy[B]): Lazy[B]`              | Chain computations                        |
| `flatten: Lazy[B]`                                  | Flatten nested `Lazy`                     |
| `as[B](b: => B): Lazy[B]`                           | Replace the value with a constant         |
| `unit: Lazy[Unit]`                                  | Discard the value                         |
| `zip[B](that: Lazy[B]): Lazy[(A, B)]`               | Combine with another `Lazy`               |
| `catchAll[B >: A](f: Throwable => Lazy[B]): Lazy[B]` | Handle errors                            |
| `ensuring(finalizer: Lazy[Any]): Lazy[A]`           | Run finalizer on completion               |
```

author: Copilot
body:
The docs state that once a `Lazy` is evaluated its result is cached and `isEvaluated` becomes true. In the current implementation, memoization uses `null` as the sentinel for “not evaluated”, so a `Lazy` that evaluates to `null` will be recomputed on every `force` and `isEvaluated` will remain false. Please clarify this limitation in the docs (or adjust the implementation to use a dedicated sentinel if `null` results should be supported).
```suggestion
Once a `Lazy` computation is evaluated, the result is cached and `isEvaluated` becomes `true`. Subsequent calls to `force` return the cached value without re-executing the computation, as long as the computed result is not `null`.

Note: the current implementation uses `null` internally as the sentinel for “not evaluated”. If a `Lazy` computation legitimately returns `null`, it will be recomputed on every `force` call and `isEvaluated` will remain `false`. To benefit from memoization, prefer non-null results (for example, use `Option[A]` instead of returning `null`).
```

author: Copilot
body:
API table entry for `flatten` is a bit misleading: in the implementation it requires evidence that `A <:< Lazy[B]` (i.e., it only works when the `Lazy` already contains a `Lazy`). Please reflect that constraint in the signature/description so readers don’t assume `flatten` is always available.
```suggestion
| Method                                                    | Description                                               |
|-----------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------|
| `force: A`                                                | Evaluate and return the result (memoized)                 |
| `isEvaluated: Boolean`                                    | Check if the value has been computed                      |
| `map[B](f: A => B): Lazy[B]`                              | Transform the value                                       |
| `flatMap[B](f: A => Lazy[B]): Lazy[B]`                    | Chain computations                                        |
| `flatten[B](implicit ev: A <:< Lazy[B]): Lazy[B]`         | Flatten nested `Lazy` when this `Lazy` contains `Lazy[B]` |
| `as[B](b: => B): Lazy[B]`                                 | Replace the value with a constant                         |
| `unit: Lazy[Unit]`                                        | Discard the value                                         |
| `zip[B](that: Lazy[B]): Lazy[(A, B)]`                     | Combine with another `Lazy`                               |
| `catchAll(f: Throwable => Lazy[B]): Lazy[B]`              | Handle errors                                             |
```

author: Copilot
body:
In the comparison table, the “Stack safety” row for Scala `lazy val` is a bit ambiguous because `lazy val` doesn’t model chained computations the way `Lazy.flatMap` does. It may be clearer to mark this as “N/A” or clarify that stack safety here refers specifically to `Lazy`’s trampolined `flatMap`/continuation evaluation.
```suggestion
| Feature            | `Lazy[A]`                    | Scala `lazy val`             |
|--------------------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Monadic operations | Yes (`map`, `flatMap`)       | No                           |
| Error handling     | Yes (`catchAll`, `ensuring`) | No                           |
| Stack safety       | Yes (trampolined `flatMap`)  | N/A (no monadic chaining)    |
| Composable         | Yes                          | Limited                      |
```

author: Copilot
body:
In “Equality and Hashing”, it’s worth noting that `hashCode` forces the value and then calls `.hashCode` on it; if a `Lazy` can evaluate to `null` (which the doc mentions earlier), `hashCode` will throw a `NullPointerException` and repeated equality checks may also re-run the thunk due to the `null` sentinel. Adding a short note here would make the semantics accurate for the `null` edge case.
```suggestion
Note: Comparing `Lazy` values will force their evaluation. Also, `hashCode` forces the value and then calls `.hashCode` on it; if a `Lazy` evaluates to `null`, `hashCode` will throw a `NullPointerException`, and repeated equality checks may re-run the thunk because `null` is used internally as the “not yet evaluated” sentinel.
```

author: Copilot
body:
`Lazy.collectAll` / `Lazy.foreach` are described as working on a generic “collection”, but the actual signatures accept `IndexedSeq` specifically. The docs should either say `IndexedSeq` explicitly or mention converting other collection types (e.g., via `.toIndexedSeq`) before calling these helpers.
```suggestion
| Method                    | Description                                                         |
|---------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `Lazy(expr: => A)`        | Create a `Lazy` from a by-name expression                          |
| `Lazy.fail(e: Throwable)` | Create a `Lazy` that fails with the given exception                |
| `Lazy.collectAll(values)` | Combine an `IndexedSeq` of `Lazy` into a single `Lazy`             |
| `Lazy.foreach(values)(f)` | Apply a function to each element of an `IndexedSeq`, collecting results |

Note: For other collection types, convert them using `.toIndexedSeq` before calling `Lazy.collectAll` or `Lazy.foreach`.
```

body:
This introduces an implicit `JsonBinaryCodec[OpenAPI]` derived from `Schema`, which (per the PR description) is *not* spec-compliant for OpenAPI JSON and may be accidentally picked up by consumers who expect the “spec-compliant JSON codec” behavior. Consider removing this implicit, moving it behind a more explicit import (e.g. `OpenAPI.DynamicJsonCodec`), or making it delegate to the spec-compliant `OpenAPICodec` layer to avoid surprising serialization behavior.
```suggestion
  /**
   * A non-spec-compliant JSON codec for OpenAPI that is derived directly from
   * the schema. This codec is intentionally not exposed as a top-level implicit
   * to avoid being accidentally selected by consumers who expect the
   * spec-compliant OpenAPI JSON representation.
   *
   * To use this codec, explicitly import:
   *
   *   import zio.blocks.openapi.OpenAPI.DynamicJsonCodec._
   */
  object DynamicJsonCodec {
    implicit lazy val jsonCodec: JsonBinaryCodec[OpenAPI] =
      schema.derive(JsonBinaryCodecDeriver)
  }
```

author: Copilot
body: Attribute decoding uses `valueType == 0` as 'store raw string', but many non-String primitives/codecs appear to default to `objectType` (0) (e.g., `BigInt`, `BigDecimal`, time types in this file). If any of those are marked as `xml.attribute`, decode will incorrectly store a `String` into the record register, leading to runtime type mismatch during construction. A safer approach is to decode attribute text through the codec itself (e.g., wrap as `<value>...</value>` and call `codec.decodeValue`) and then store the result using the appropriate register setter for `codec.valueType` (object/int/long/etc.).

author: Copilot
body: Variant encoding always wraps the encoded case value inside a `<caseName>...</caseName>` element, even when the case codec already encodes to an element named `caseName` (common for record cases). This can lead to duplicated nesting (`<Dog><Dog>...</Dog></Dog>`) and unnecessary structural noise. Consider detecting `inner` as `Xml.Element` with matching name and returning it directly (or extracting its children) to avoid double-wrapping.

author: Copilot
body: `String#getBytes(String)` depends on the encoding name being supported and can throw at runtime (and behaves differently across platforms). Since `WriterConfig.encoding` is user-provided, consider using `java.nio.charset.Charset.forName(config.encoding)` (JVM) and/or restricting/validating supported encodings so failures are deterministic and easier to diagnose. The Scala.js writer has the same pattern and should be kept consistent.

author: Copilot
body:
Namespace serialization is incorrect for prefixed names. If `name.prefix` is defined and `name.namespace` is defined, emitting `xmlns=\"...\"` declares the *default* namespace, but the element is written with a prefix, so the namespace binding should be `xmlns:prefix=\"...\"`. Consider branching on `name.prefix`: emit `xmlns=\"...\"` only when there is no prefix, otherwise emit `xmlns:<prefix>=\"...\"`.
```suggestion
      sb.append(" xmlns")
      name.prefix.foreach { prefix =>
        sb.append(':')
        sb.append(prefix)
      }
      sb.append("=\"")
```

Constructing `prefixedName` via `XmlName(s\"$prefix:$recordName\")` stores the prefix inside `localName` (with `prefix = None`), which breaks invariants assumed elsewhere (e.g., `XmlName.localName` should be prefix-free). This can also make APIs like `XmlSelection.get(\"Item\")` fail when the encoded AST contains `localName = \"ex:Item\"`. Prefer building `XmlName(recordName, prefix = Some(prefix), namespace = None)` (and let the writer emit the correct qualified name), while keeping `localName` prefix-free.
```suggestion
                val prefixedName = XmlName(recordName, prefix = Some(prefix), namespace = None)
                val nsAttr       = (XmlName("xmlns", prefix = Some(prefix), namespace = None), uri)
```

This conflicts with `XmlWriter.writeToBytes`, which supports arbitrary `WriterConfig.encoding`; if users write UTF-16 bytes, `readFromBytes` will mis-decode. Consider either (1) accepting an explicit charset/encoding parameter, (2) detecting BOM / parsing the XML declaration encoding, or (3) documenting that byte decoding is UTF-8-only and aligning `WriterConfig.encoding` behavior accordingly.

author: Copilot
body:
`PlatformScope.threadYield()` on Scala.js throws an exception, but `Resource.Shared` calls `PlatformScope.threadYield()` in the `Pending` state spin loop. Even on JS (single-threaded), `Pending` can be observed via re-entrant initialization (e.g., `makeFn` calling `make` on the same shared resource), which would then crash. Consider making `threadYield()` a no-op on JS (or implement a safer backoff strategy in `Resource.Shared` that doesn't rely on `Thread.yield`).
```suggestion
  def threadYield(): Unit = ()
```

author: Copilot
body: The Scaladoc example appears out of date with the new API: it references `$[Config]`, `config $ identity`, `defer(...)`, and returns `Context[Database](db)`, but the new design removed the top-level `$` accessor and `Wire.Shared` now constructs `Out` directly via `(Finalizer, Context[In]) => Out` rather than returning a `Context`. Please update the example to match the current public API so it compiles and demonstrates the intended usage (e.g., use the provided `Finalizer`/`ctx.get[...]` and return `db` directly).

author: Copilot
body: The comment for `extractScopeHasType` describes the "new design" as an HList-based `Scope.::`/`Scope.Has` encoding, but this PR replaces that with `Scope[ParentTag, Tag]` and deprecates `InStack`/stack lookup. If this method is still needed, the documentation (and likely the pattern matching) should be updated to reflect the current `Scope` type structure; otherwise consider removing it to avoid misleading future macro maintenance.

author: Copilot
body:
`scoped` claims to create a child scope with a fresh/existential tag, but the implementation constructs `childScope` as `new Scope[self.Tag, self.Tag](...)`, making the child tag equal to the parent tag. That defeats the compile-time leak-prevention guarantee (child-allocated values will be tagged with the parent tag and can be used in the parent scope). Create a fresh local tag type (e.g., `type Fresh <: self.Tag`) and instantiate the child as `Scope[self.Tag, Fresh]` so values allocated in the child cannot typecheck in the parent.
```suggestion
    type Fresh <: self.Tag
    val childScope: Scope[self.Tag, Fresh] = new Scope[self.Tag, Fresh](new Finalizers)
    var primary: Throwable | Null         = null
```

author: Copilot
body:
Same issue as Scala 3: `scoped` constructs `childScope` with type parameters `Scope[self.Tag, self.Tag]`, so the child tag is not fresh/existential and equals the parent tag. This breaks the intended compile-time escape prevention (child-allocated values become usable in the parent scope). Use a fresh local tag type and construct `Scope[self.Tag, Fresh]`, passing it to `f` as `Scope[self.Tag, _ <: self.Tag]`.
```suggestion
    // Fresh, invocation-specific child tag type to prevent compile-time escapes
    final class FreshTagMarker
    type ChildTag = self.Tag with FreshTagMarker

    val childScope: Scope[self.Tag, ChildTag] = new Scope[self.Tag, ChildTag](new Finalizers)
    var primary: Throwable                    = null.asInstanceOf[Throwable]
```

author: Copilot
body: `createTestableScope()` returns a scope tagged with `Scope.GlobalTag`, but this scope *does* close. Because `ScopeEscape` treats `GlobalTag` as always-escaping, this allows any resourceful type allocated in the test scope to escape as a raw value, contradicting the semantics documented above (GlobalTag is supposed to be safe because the global scope never closes). Consider giving test scopes a non-global tag (e.g., return `Scope[GlobalTag, _ <: GlobalTag]` with a fresh local tag) so `ScopeEscape.globalScope` does not apply.

author: Copilot
body: `Resource.from[T]` (no-overrides variant) only detects an implicit `Scope[?, ?]` parameter (`hasScopeParam`), but Finalizer injection is a key feature and an implicit `Finalizer` parameter will not be detected or passed here. This makes `Resource.from[T]` fail to compile for classes that take an implicit `Finalizer` (or require it for cleanup registration). Update detection/codegen to recognize `Finalizer` parameters in implicit/given param lists and pass the `finalizer` provided by `Resource.shared` to the constructor.

author: Copilot
body: `resolveNewtypeQualifier` currently triggers for *any* `TypeRef` whose referenced symbol is owned by a Newtype/Subtype base, but it doesn’t verify that the referenced member is actually the newtype’s `Type` (as described in the scaladoc). If the base trait defines other type members, those could be incorrectly re-labeled to the qualifier term name/owner and yield wrong `TypeId`s. Consider guarding on `tr.typeSymbol.name == "Type"` (or otherwise restricting the match to the intended newtype type member) before returning `Some(...)`.

author: Copilot
body: `TransformElements` / `TransformKeys` / `TransformValues` currently evaluate the transform once and then replace *every* element/key/value with the same `transformValue`. That breaks the expected semantics of “transform each element/key/value” (the expression should be evaluated per element/key/value, with that element/key/value as the expression input). Consider changing these actions to either (a) store a `DynamicSchemaExpr` and evaluate it per item (via `modifyAtPath` over `Node.Elements` / `Node.MapKeys` / `Node.MapValues`), or (b) keep the action shape but pass the original item into `evalExpr` for each iteration instead of precomputing a constant.

author: Copilot
body: `executeJoin` appends `(fieldName -> combinedValue)` unconditionally, which can create duplicate field names in `DynamicValue.Record`. Since other code uses `indexWhere` to locate fields, duplicates can lead to surprising behavior (only the first matching field is found/updated). Suggestion: if the field already exists, either overwrite it via `updated(idx, ...)` or return `SchemaError.fieldAlreadyExists(at, fieldName)` (consistent with `AddField`).

author: Copilot
body: `executeSplit` appends new fields for each `targetPath` without checking whether a target field already exists, which can introduce duplicate keys in `DynamicValue.Record` and cause ambiguous field lookups later. Suggestion: for each target, detect existing `fieldName` and either overwrite in place or fail with `SchemaError.fieldAlreadyExists(targetPath, fieldName)`.

author: Copilot
body:
`Optionalize.reverse` uses `DynamicValue.Record.empty` as the default when converting back to a required field. This will be invalid for most non-record types (e.g., `Int`, `String`) and will reliably cause type mismatch/conversion errors at runtime. Consider either: (1) making `Optionalize` carry an explicit `defaultForReverse: DynamicSchemaExpr` (mirroring `DropField`), or (2) making `reverse` best-effort by returning `Optionalize(at)` (i.e., not attempting a lossy structural inverse without a valid default).
```suggestion
    override def reverse: MigrationAction = Optionalize(at)
```

author: Copilot
body: The `asInstanceOf[NumericPrimitiveType[A]]` casts are avoidable and weaken type safety (and can hide bugs if `IsNumeric` is ever extended beyond numeric primitives). Since `IsNumeric` now provides `numericPrimitiveType: NumericPrimitiveType[A]`, you can pass `isNumeric.numericPrimitiveType` directly and remove the cast (apply similarly to `*`, `/`, `%`, and `pow`).

author: Copilot
body: `new Schema(this.source)` is allocated on every operator call (`===`, `>`, `&&`, arithmetic ops, bitwise ops, string ops, etc.). If these operators are used in hot paths or large expression builders, this can create avoidable allocation pressure. Consider storing/caching a `Schema[S]` on the optic (or providing a reusable `def sourceSchema: Schema[S]`) and reusing it across operator methods instead of constructing a new `Schema` each time.

author: Copilot
body: Several builder operations accept both a `from` selector (source) and a `to` selector (target), but the generated actions only use the source path at runtime (e.g., `TransformValue(fromPath, ...)` here). If a caller passes different `from`/`to` fields, the resulting migration will not populate/rename the target field even though type tracking marks it as provided. Consider either (1) enforcing `fromFieldName == toFieldName` in the macro and aborting compilation with a clear error when they differ, or (2) extending the action model/executor to carry and apply the `to` path (i.e., true source→target mapping). This same concern applies to `mandateFieldImpl`, `optionalizeFieldImpl`, and `changeFieldTypeImpl`.

author: Copilot
body: Same issue as Scala 3 macros: `toPath` is computed only for validation/type-tracking, but the emitted runtime action (`TransformValue(fromPath, ...)`) ignores it. This can mislead users into thinking `transformField(from, to, ...)` maps into `to`, when it only mutates `from`. Consider adding a macro-time check that `fromFieldName == toFieldName` (and abort otherwise), or updating the action/executor to support true from→to mapping.

author: Copilot
body: These tests (and many others in this spec) construct expressions and then assert `assertTrue(true)`, which doesn’t verify behavior and can allow regressions to slip through. Also, there are duplicated suites (e.g., two `suite(\"Not\")`) and duplicated test names elsewhere. Suggestion: replace placeholder assertions with `evalDynamic` (or `dynamic` AST) assertions that check actual results, and deduplicate/restructure repeated suites/tests to keep the spec meaningful and maintainable.

body: `c.inferImplicitValue(..., silent = true)` returns `EmptyTree` both when no implicit is found and when implicit resolution fails due to ambiguity/divergence. As written, ambiguous `Schema[T]` instances will be silently ignored in favor of `Schema.derived[T]`. Recommendation: adjust the logic to distinguish ‘not found’ from other implicit-resolution failures (or explicitly re-trigger implicit search in a way that preserves the compiler error for ambiguity) so ambiguity isn’t masked.

Consider asserting directly on the `Either` with matchers (as used elsewhere in this file) so the diff is clearer when it fails (e.g., `isRight(...)` with an explicit predicate over the `DynamicValue`).

author: Copilot
body:
Filtering out `Product` / `Equals` / `Serializable` / `Mirror.*` from `TypeDefKind` base types will also change `TypeId.parents` and `TypeId.isSubtypeOf` semantics (subtyping is computed via `defKind.baseTypes`). E.g. a case class will no longer be considered a subtype of `java.io.Serializable` / `scala.Product` even though it is on the JVM. If this behavior change is intended for bytecode size, it should be documented and covered by tests; otherwise consider keeping semantic subtyping accurate by special-casing these well-known supertypes in the subtyping logic while omitting them from the stored baseTypes for codegen size.
```suggestion
    "scala.Matchable"
```

author: Copilot
body: `isFilteredBaseClass` compares `base == sym`, but in the `sym.isModule` branches you pass the *module term symbol* while `baseClasses` contains *class symbols* (including `sym.asModule.moduleClass`). This means the module’s own class likely won’t be filtered and can end up in its own `bases` list. Consider normalizing `sym` to the moduleClass when `sym.isModule` (and similarly when `sym.isModuleClass`) before doing the equality check, so `base == selfClassSym` is properly excluded.

author: Copilot
body:
Same self-filtering issue applies to `buildBaseTypesMinimal`: in the `sym.isModule` branch `base == sym` will be false for the module’s own class symbol, so it can be included in `baseClasses` and emitted into `bases` unnecessarily. Fixing `isFilteredBaseClass` as suggested will address this call site too.
```suggestion
      val moduleClassSym = sym.asModule.moduleClass
      moduleClassSym.asClass.baseClasses.filterNot(isFilteredBaseClass(c)(_, moduleClassSym))
```

If `scala.Serializable` isn’t filtered, it may still appear in generated baseTypes and reduce the effectiveness of this change. Consider adding `"scala.Serializable"` to the filtered set.

Adding a focused test (e.g., asserting a derived case class’s `defKind.baseTypes` includes only user-defined parents) would help prevent future regressions that reintroduce `Method too large` failures.

This can make `migration.reverse` fail at runtime even when the original (source) schema has a default for that field. Resolve `defaultForReverse` best-effort at build time (similar to `DropField`) or ensure defaults are resolved when constructing the reversed migration.

      case MigrationAction.Optionalize(at, defaultForReverse) =>

```

author: Copilot
body:
The `$` operator in Scala 2 doesn't enforce scope tag capability checking. The Scala 3 version requires `Scope[?] { type Tag >: S }` which ensures the scope's Tag is a supertype of the scoped value's tag `S`. However, the Scala 2 version only requires `Scope.Any`, which doesn't check the tag relationship at all. This means the Scala 2 implementation doesn't provide the same compile-time safety guarantees as Scala 3 - resources could potentially be accessed with the wrong scope in context without compiler errors. Consider adding a refined type bound or documenting this limitation.
```suggestion
  def $[B](f: A => B)(implicit @unused scope: Scope { type Tag >: S }, u: AutoUnscoped[B, S]): u.Out =
```

author: Copilot
body:
The Tag type bound `type Tag >: ParentTag` is less precise than Scala 3's union type `ParentTag | this.type`. This allows any supertype of ParentTag rather than specifically the union. While this is a limitation of Scala 2 (which lacks union types), it means the Scala 2 version provides weaker type safety. The documentation at docs/scope.md:1010 mentions this difference but doesn't clearly explain the safety implications. Consider adding a note in the documentation about this limitation and its potential impact on compile-time safety.
```suggestion

  /**
   * NOTE: In Scala 3 the corresponding definition is:
   *
   *   type Tag = ParentTag | this.type
   *
   * Since Scala 2 does not support union types, we approximate that
   * definition with a lower type bound:
   *
   *   type Tag >: ParentTag
   *
   * This is intentionally less precise: it permits any supertype of
   * `ParentTag`, not specifically the union `ParentTag | this.type`.
   * As a result, the Scala 2 implementation provides slightly weaker
   * compile-time safety than the Scala 3 version, even though the
   * runtime semantics are intended to be the same.
   */
```

author: Copilot
body:
The test creates scoped values using `@@.scoped` with a `String` tag, but then uses these values in operations without any scope being in context. The `$` operator in Scala 2 requires `implicit scope: Scope.Any`.

While the Scala 2 `$` operator doesn't enforce tag capability checking like Scala 3 does (it only requires `Scope.Any`), the test is still misleading because:
1. It creates artificial scoped values not from an actual scope
2. It doesn't demonstrate realistic usage patterns
3. The assertion `assertTrue(result != null)` is weak and doesn't validate the behavior

The test should either use scoped values from an actual scope or add a comment explaining that this tests the type algebra in isolation.

author: Copilot
body:
The "Problem" example uses the new API syntax (`$[Request] $ (_.body)`) to demonstrate a problem that existed with the old API. This is confusing because:

1. In the old API (before this PR), `$[Request]` returned raw `Request`, so escaping would be: `leaked = $[Request].body` (no `$` operator needed)
2. In the new API (after this PR), `$[Request]` returns `Request @@ closeable.Tag`, and `$[Request] $ (_.body)` returns `InputStream @@ closeable.Tag` (not raw `InputStream`), so the assignment `leaked = $[Request] $ (_.body)` would fail to compile

The example should either:
- Show the old API code that demonstrates the problem: `leaked = scope.get[Request].body` or similar
- Update the comment to clarify this is pseudo-code representing the conceptual problem, not actual compilable code
- Make it clear this is showing what you WOULD do if scoping wasn't enforced
```suggestion
**The Problem**: With a pre-opaque API (before the tagging shown below), even with type-level stack tracking, resources could escape their scope:

```scala
var leaked: InputStream = null

Scope.global.injected[Request].run { implicit scope =>
  leaked = scope.get[Request].body  // Captured reference survives scope!
```

The Scala 2 test creates scoped values using `@@.scoped` with a `String` tag, but then uses these values in operations (`flatMap`, `map`) without any scope being in context. The `$` operator in Scala 2 requires `implicit scope: Scope.Any`, but there's no scope in context that would satisfy the tag requirement.

While the test currently passes (likely because it only verifies `assertTrue(true)`), the test doesn't properly demonstrate the scoping mechanism. The test should either:
1. Create a proper scope and use scoped values from that scope
2. Add an implicit scope that can be used with these manually-created scoped values
3. Document that manual creation with `@@.scoped` is for internal testing only

author: Copilot
body:
The test creates scoped values using `@@.scoped` with a `String` tag, but these are artificial test values not created from an actual scope. While the test verifies that `map` works on scoped values, it doesn't verify that the scope tag mechanism works correctly.

In Scala 3, the `$` operator requires `using scope: Scope[?] { type Tag >: S }`, which means accessing these String-tagged values would require a scope whose Tag is a supertype of String. Since no such scope exists in the test, if the test tried to use the `$` operator, it would fail to compile.

The test should either:
1. Use scoped values from an actual scope to demonstrate realistic usage
2. Add a comment explaining that this is testing the type algebra of scoped values in isolation
3. Actually attempt to use `$` operator (which should fail) to demonstrate the safety guarantee

author: Copilot
body:
The `sortKeys` method in `Array` could benefit from a reference equality optimization similar to the one used in `Object.sortKeys` (lines 500-505). While `Array.sortKeys` doesn't filter elements, using the reference equality check can still help avoid unnecessary work when child elements are unchanged. The pattern should be:
```
val v1 = value(idx)
val v2 = v1.sortKeys
arr(idx) = if (v1 eq v2) v1 else v2
```
This optimization is used consistently in `Object.sortKeys`, `Object.dropNulls`, `Object.dropEmpty`, and `Object.normalize` for the same purpose - to avoid unnecessary allocations when recursive calls return the same instance.
```suggestion
        val v1 = value(idx)
        val v2 = v1.sortKeys
        arr(idx) = if (v1 eq v2) v1 else v2
```

            .instance
            .force
        }
```

body:
Consider extracting this type mapping into a private Map or using a helper method to reduce duplication. The same pattern appears in multiple places (e.g., analyzeTypeReprMinimal at line 423).
```suggestion
  // Centralized mapping from predefined type names to their corresponding TypeRepr expressions.
  private def predefinedTypeReprForName(name: String)(using Quotes): Option[Expr[TypeRepr]] =
    name match {
      case "Int"     => Some('{ TypeRepr.Ref(TypeId.int) })
      case "String"  => Some('{ TypeRepr.Ref(TypeId.string) })
      case "Long"    => Some('{ TypeRepr.Ref(TypeId.long) })
      case "Boolean" => Some('{ TypeRepr.Ref(TypeId.boolean) })
      case "Double"  => Some('{ TypeRepr.Ref(TypeId.double) })
      case "Float"   => Some('{ TypeRepr.Ref(TypeId.float) })
      case "Byte"    => Some('{ TypeRepr.Ref(TypeId.byte) })
      case "Short"   => Some('{ TypeRepr.Ref(TypeId.short) })
      case "Char"    => Some('{ TypeRepr.Ref(TypeId.char) })
      case "Unit"    => Some('{ TypeRepr.Ref(TypeId.unit) })
      case "List"    => Some('{ TypeRepr.Ref(TypeId.list) })
      case "Option"  => Some('{ TypeRepr.Ref(TypeId.option) })
      case "Map"     => Some('{ TypeRepr.Ref(TypeId.map) })
      case "Either"  => Some('{ TypeRepr.Ref(TypeId.either) })
      case "Set"     => Some('{ TypeRepr.Ref(TypeId.set) })
      case "Vector"  => Some('{ TypeRepr.Ref(TypeId.vector) })
      case _         => None
    }

  private def typeReprDataToExpr(data: TypeReprData)(using Quotes): Expr[TypeRepr] = data match {
    case TypeReprData.PredefinedData(name) =>
      predefinedTypeReprForName(name).getOrElse {
        '{ TypeRepr.Ref(TypeId.nominal[Nothing](${ Expr(name) }, Owner.Root, Nil)) }
```

body:
The magic numbers (0x100000000L, 0x200000000L, etc.) lack explanation. Add comments documenting what these hexadecimal values represent in the register offset encoding scheme, or extract them as named constants to improve code readability.
```suggestion
        // Offsets are encoded so that the low 32 bits count object registers (step = 1L),
        // while different ranges in the high bits count primitive register slots.
        // Each primitive slot corresponds to one step of (1L << 32) in its bit range.
        // 0x100000000L == 1L << 32, 0x200000000L == 1L << 33, etc.
        val BooleanOrByteRegisterDelta: Long = 0x100000000L // one boolean/byte slot
        val ShortOrCharRegisterDelta: Long   = 0x200000000L // one short/char slot
        val IntOrFloatRegisterDelta: Long    = 0x400000000L // one int/float slot
        val LongOrDoubleRegisterDelta: Long  = 0x800000000L // one long/double slot
        val ObjectRegisterDelta: Long        = 1L           // one object reference slot

        def offsetDelta(registerType: String): Long = registerType match {
          case "boolean" => BooleanOrByteRegisterDelta
          case "byte"    => BooleanOrByteRegisterDelta
          case "short"   => ShortOrCharRegisterDelta
          case "int"     => IntOrFloatRegisterDelta
          case "long"    => LongOrDoubleRegisterDelta
          case "float"   => IntOrFloatRegisterDelta
          case "double"  => LongOrDoubleRegisterDelta
          case "char"    => ShortOrCharRegisterDelta
          case "object"  => ObjectRegisterDelta
```

body:
Obviously this needs tests, but we must have a guarantee everything has been reassociated to the left, so we end up with a type `Either[A, B]` where `A` may be an `Either`, but `B` is definitely **NOT** an `Either`.

As for semantics:

1. combine must always return the reassociated form
2. separate must always FIRST reassociate and then extract (top-level)

These provide very strong guarantees that best what we did elsewhere in the ZIO ecosystem.

The `StringSplitToFields` transform uses a default empty string for missing parts (line 153). This means if the input string doesn't split into enough parts, the remaining fields are populated with empty strings. This could be problematic if the schema expects non-empty strings or if empty strings have special meaning. Consider either returning an error when there aren't enough parts, or making the default value configurable.
```suggestion
  final case class StringSplitToFields(
    fieldNames:   Vector[String],
    separator:    String,
    limit:        Int     = -1,
    defaultValue: String  = ""
  ) extends DynamicValueTransform {
    def apply(value: DynamicValue): Either[String, DynamicValue] = value match {
      case DynamicValue.Primitive(PrimitiveValue.String(s)) =>
        val parts  = if (limit > 0) s.split(separator, limit) else s.split(separator)
        val fields = fieldNames.zipWithIndex.map { case (name, idx) =>
          val part = if (idx < parts.length) parts(idx) else defaultValue
```

This is inconsistent with the expectation that this action is converting from Optional to required. If a field is already required (not wrapped in Option/Some), this silently does nothing, which could mask migration configuration errors. Consider returning an error when the field is not in the expected Optional format.

        val (_, value) = fields(idx)
        val unwrappedEither: Either[MigrationError, DynamicValue] =

            case DynamicValue.Variant("None", _) =>

The validation logic only checks added, removed, and renamed fields, which could lead to false positives when fields are transformed in place. Consider extending the validation to account for field transformations.

author: Copilot
body:
This command has operator precedence issues that cause unintended behavior. Due to how shell operators bind, this parses as: `(sleep 10 && gh pr checks ... > /dev/null 2>&1) || (true && gh pr checks <PR>)`. This means the final status display will always run (since `true &&` always succeeds), even when unintended.

Additionally, the `sleep 10` at the beginning delays the start of watching by 10 seconds, which seems unnecessary.

Consider using one of these alternatives:
- Simple sequential execution: `gh pr checks <PR> --watch --fail-fast > /dev/null 2>&1; gh pr checks <PR>`
- With proper grouping to ignore watch failures: `(gh pr checks <PR> --watch --fail-fast > /dev/null 2>&1 || true) && gh pr checks <PR>`

Both alternatives achieve the goal of suppressing watch output while displaying final status, without the operator precedence issues or unnecessary delay.
```suggestion
`(gh pr checks <PR> --watch --fail-fast > /dev/null 2>&1 || true) && gh pr checks <PR>`
```

          else Right(Some(ChunkMap.from(results.collect { case Right(kv) => kv })))

body: There's a mismatch between the setup instructions and the command template. Line 7 says to place sbtn at `.git/bin/sbtn` and add `.git/bin` to PATH. However, line 27 runs `sbt --client`. For this to work with sbtn, you would need either: (1) a symlink from `.git/bin/sbt` to `.git/bin/sbtn`, or (2) to rename sbtn to sbt, or (3) change the command to use `sbtn --client`. body: The asInstanceOf cast from BigDecimal to BigInt is unsafe and will cause a ClassCastException at runtime. JsonSchema stores minimum/maximum as BigDecimal, but when A is BigInt, the direct cast will fail. Instead, convert the BigDecimal to BigInt using .toBigInt() when the type parameter is BigInt, or use a type-specific conversion strategy.

author: Copilot
body: The buildStringValidation method has an inconsistent handling of minLength when it equals 0. When minLength=0 and maxLength is Some, it correctly creates a Length validation (line 254-255). However, when minLength=0 and maxLength=None, or when minLength=0 and maxLength is also 0, the case is not handled and falls through to Validation.None (line 258-259). This is inconsistent because minLength=0 is typically meaningless but the method handles it differently depending on whether maxLength is present.

author: Copilot

The unwrap function in wrapPrimitive silently returns default values when primitiveType.fromDynamicValue fails (line 299). This can hide validation errors and lead to unexpected behavior. For example, if a string primitive type has validation constraints and the DynamicValue fails validation, the unwrap will silently return an empty string instead of propagating the error. This could cause data loss in round-trip scenarios where invalid data is silently replaced with defaults.
```suggestion
          case Right(a)    => a
          case Left(error) =>

```

author: Copilot
body: The buildRecord function receives a 'required' parameter (line 321) but marks it as unused and does not implement any logic to distinguish between required and optional fields. According to the PR description, "all defined properties are now required (optional field handling TBD)". This means that the current implementation treats all fields as required regardless of the JsonSchema's 'required' array. While this is documented as a known limitation, it represents incomplete functionality that could cause unexpected validation behavior. Users might expect optional fields to be validated according to the JsonSchema specification.

body: The buildFieldVariant function ignores its discriminator parameter (line 571) and simply delegates to buildKeyVariant (line 574). This means that field-discriminated variants (which have a common discriminator field like "type") are treated identically to key-discriminated variants (which use wrapper objects). This could lead to incorrect JSON encoding/decoding behavior for field-discriminated variants, as the discriminator field information is lost.

author: Copilot
body:
The buildStringValidation method does not handle the case where both a pattern and min/max length constraints are present simultaneously. When a JsonSchema has both pattern and length constraints (e.g., minLength=5, maxLength=10, pattern="^[a-z]+$"), the method will fall through to the default case and return Validation.None, ignoring all constraints. This loses validation information.

Consider handling this case by either:
1. Creating a combined validation that applies both pattern and length checks
2. Prioritizing one constraint type with a documented preference
3. Returning the pattern validation and documenting that length constraints are ignored when pattern is present

The wrap and unwrap functions are essentially identity operations (wrap = dv => Right(dv), unwrap = identity). The `buildOption` implementation wraps the inner schema with an identity wrapper that doesn't actually implement optional semantics. The wrap/unwrap functions both pass through the DynamicValue unchanged. This means that nullable types (e.g., `["string", "null"]`) are not being properly handled as Option types - they're just wrapped without any null-handling logic.

This could lead to issues where null values are not properly converted to None, or where the presence/absence of a value isn't correctly distinguished. The implementation should include logic to convert null DynamicValues to Option.empty and non-null values to Option(value), similar to how other bindings handle optional values.
