```suggestion
                  s"Unexpected primitive type in wrapPrimitive error recovery: ${other.getClass.getName}"
                )
```

author: Copilot
body: The `buildOption` method creates a trivial wrapper that doesn't actually implement Option semantics (Some/None distinction). This is documented as a known limitation in the PR description, but the implementation may be misleading. The wrapper simply passes through the inner value without handling null/None cases. Consider adding a comment explaining this limitation directly in the code, or renaming the shape to avoid confusion.

author: Copilot
body: The key-discriminated variant analysis only accepts cases where the required field exactly matches the single property. However, the logic doesn't verify that `additionalProperties` is false. This means if a case has `additionalProperties: true` or some schema, it would still be classified as key-discriminated variant, but the extra properties wouldn't be properly handled. Consider adding a check for closed records or document this behavior.

author: Copilot
body: The `toReflect` method recursively processes JsonSchema without cycle detection. If a JsonSchema contains recursive references (e.g., a schema that references itself through properties or array items), this could lead to infinite recursion and stack overflow. Consider implementing cycle detection or documenting that recursive schemas are not supported in this version.

author: Copilot
body:
The field order in records is determined by the iteration order of `Map.toList`. In Scala, the iteration order of Map is not guaranteed to be stable across different executions or Scala versions (though it may be deterministic within a single execution). This could cause inconsistent field ordering which may affect serialization and cross-platform compatibility. Consider sorting fields by name or using a LinkedHashMap/ListMap to preserve insertion order.
```suggestion
      val props    = obj.properties.getOrElse(Map.empty).toList.sortBy(_._1)
```

author: Copilot
body: The JsonSchemaToReflect object lacks documentation. Given the complexity of this 633-line implementation with multiple shape analysis functions and builders, it should have scaladoc comments explaining the overall purpose, the conversion strategy, and the mapping table shown in the PR description. Following the pattern seen in ContextDetector.scala, add documentation for the main object and key methods like `toReflect` and `analyze`.

author: Copilot
body: This test consumes one element from `ci` via `nextAt(0)` and then calls `ci.sliceIterator(...)` on the same iterator. That breaks for stateful `ChunkIterator` implementations (e.g., the `ChunkIterator.Iterator` used by `VectorChunk`), because `nextAt` advances the underlying iterator. To make the round-trip robust, avoid consuming `ci` before slicing (or iterate the full iterator without mixing it with `sliceIterator`).

author: Copilot
body:
These assertions compare `getClass.toString` to hard-coded class name strings. This is brittle across Scala versions / platforms (JVM vs JS/Native) and can break with shading/relocation. Prefer asserting on `getClass.getName` and using a suffix/contains check (e.g., endsWith("Chunk$IntArray")), or validate observable behavior instead of exact class names.
```suggestion
        assert(Chunk.fromIterable(Array("a")).getClass.getName)(endsWithString("Chunk$AnyRefArray")) &&
        assert(Chunk.fromIterable(Array(1)).getClass.getName)(endsWithString("Chunk$IntArray")) &&
        assert(Chunk.fromIterable(Array(1L)).getClass.getName)(endsWithString("Chunk$LongArray")) &&
        assert(Chunk.fromIterable(Array(1.0f)).getClass.getName)(endsWithString("Chunk$FloatArray")) &&
        assert(Chunk.fromIterable(Array(1.0)).getClass.getName)(endsWithString("Chunk$DoubleArray")) &&
        assert(Chunk.fromIterable(Array(1, "a")).getClass.getName)(endsWithString("Chunk$AnyRefArray"))
```

author: Copilot
body:
Changing the array-backed chunk implementation classes (e.g., `AnyRefArray`, `IntArray`, etc.) from public to `private` is a source/binary breaking API change for any downstream users who referenced these types (even just for pattern matching). If this is not intended as a breaking release, consider keeping the types accessible (e.g., `private[chunk]`), or providing public type aliases / extractors so existing code can continue to compile.
```suggestion
  private[chunk] final class AnyRefArray[A <: AnyRef](arr: Array[A], offset: Int, override val length: Int)
```

author: Copilot
body:
`addLastByte` resizes only when `idx > array.length`, but it writes to `array(idx)`. When `idx == array.length` this will throw `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` (e.g., after enough booleans are added without a prior `sizeHint`). The resize condition should be `idx >= array.length`.
```suggestion
      if (idx >= array.length) array = util.Arrays.copyOf(array, idx << 1)
```

author: Copilot
body: `mapAccum` changed parameter names (`s1`/`f1` -> `s0`/`f`). While this is behavior-preserving, it is a source-breaking change for any callers using named arguments (e.g., `mapAccum(s1 = ...)`). If maintaining source compatibility matters, consider keeping the original parameter names (or adding an overload / deprecated forwarder with the old names).

author: Copilot
body:
`takeWhile` currently returns `Chunk.empty` when the predicate holds for all elements (i.e., when the loop completes). That is incorrect: it should return the original chunk (or `take(len)`). This will make `takeWhile` drop all elements for chunks where `f` is always true and should also fail the existing `takeWhile` tests.
```suggestion
    take(len)
```

body:
Though tested indirectly by JsonPatchSpec, this needs its own test suite that is designed to test:

1. Correctness.
2. Minimalness. It's easy to just "Set" your way to the new object. What we need to do is to test that the patch is "minimal" in the sense that it's the smallest patch that brings the source object to the target object. This can be done for strings, objects, and arrays.

Ensure coverage of all statements and branches in `JsonDiffer`.

author: jdegoes
body: JsonPatch and its friends need implicit schemas so we can serialize them. The tests can roundtrip through DynamicValue, which should preserve everything, because DV is a superset of JSON.

author: jdegoes
body:
These types of tests are essential, because we must have `a.diff(b).apply(a) == b`, always.

However, for two random JSON objects, the patch will always be "clobber" / "set". So what you need to do is generate `a`, then generate tweak that into `b` by adding / removing something nested (or changing a type, etc. -- there are several different kinds of tweaks that need to be tested), and then verify the law holds. Othewise you're mainly testing the "Set" operation at the root level which is boring and wrong (insufficient).

author: Copilot
body:
The condition should use `>` instead of `>=`. When `newLen > array.length`, the array needs to grow. However, the current condition `newLen >= array.length` will grow the array even when `newLen == array.length`, which is unnecessary since the array has exactly enough space. While not critical, this causes unnecessary array copies and allocations when the new length exactly matches the current capacity.
```suggestion
        if (newLen > array.length) array = util.Arrays.copyOf(array, newLen)
```

author: Copilot
body:
The `result()` method calls `addLastByte` which modifies the builder's state by incrementing `len`. According to Scala's `Builder` contract, calling `result()` multiple times on the same builder instance should return the same result or a logically equivalent one. However, this implementation will add an extra byte each time `result()` is called, leading to incorrect results. Consider saving the state before calling `addLastByte` or checking if the last byte has already been added.
```suggestion
      // Save current state so that result() is idempotent and does not mutate the builder.
      val savedLen         = len
      val savedMaxBitIndex = maxBitIndex
      val savedLastByte    = lastByte

      addLastByte
      val bytes: Chunk[SByte] = Chunk.fromArray(Array.copyOf(array, len))
      val res                 = new Chunk.BitChunkByte(bytes, 0, 8 * (bytes.length - 1) + maxBitIndex)

      // Restore original state to respect the Builder contract.
      len         = savedLen
      maxBitIndex = savedMaxBitIndex
      lastByte    = savedLastByte

      res
```

author: Copilot
body:
The condition in `addLastByte` should check `idx >= array.length` instead of `idx > array.length`. When `idx` equals `array.length`, accessing `array(idx)` will throw an `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`. This is a critical bug that would cause the Boolean ChunkBuilder to fail when the array is full.
```suggestion
      if (idx >= array.length) array = Array.copyOf(array, idx << 1)
```

author: Copilot
body: The `equals` method calls `result()` on both `this` and `that`, which has side effects for the Boolean ChunkBuilder (see line 259-262 where `result()` modifies state via `addLastByte`). This means that calling `equals` will modify the builder's state, which violates the expected contract of `equals` being a pure, side-effect-free method. This could lead to unexpected behavior where equality checks change the builder's state.

author: Copilot
body:
In the `eitherDecoder` implementation, the error path for the Right-branch currently uses `error.atField("Left")`, which incorrectly attributes decoding failures under the `Right` key to the `Left` field. This will produce misleading error paths when decoding fails in the `Right` case. To keep error reporting accurate, the Right-branch should tag errors with the `"Right"` field name instead.
```suggestion
              case Left(error) => return new Left(error.atField("Right"))
```

author: jdegoes
body: Good catch! Fixed in 75f8c59b - this was indeed a copy-paste error.

author: jdegoes
body:
Actually, upon further review, neither Copilot nor I were correct. `Left` and `Right` are **cases** of the `Either` sum type, not fields. The correct fix is to use `atCase` instead of `atField`.

Fixed in ff6ac596:
- `eitherDecoder`: Both Left and Right branches now use `atCase`
- `optionDecoder`: Also fixed to use `atCase("Some")` when inner decoder fails

This properly represents the semantic difference between navigating into a record field (`atField`) vs navigating into a sum type case (`atCase`).

author: Copilot
body: According to the PR description and issue #873, DynamicSchema should be serializable and include a `Schema[DynamicSchema]`, with tests for round-trip serialization. However, there are no tests for serialization/deserialization of DynamicSchema itself. The issue mentions tests like "primitive schema round-trips", "record schema round-trips", etc., but these are missing from the test file. Consider adding tests to verify that DynamicSchema instances can be serialized and deserialized correctly.

author: Copilot
body:
According to the PR description and issue #873, there should be a `Schema[DynamicSchema]` defined in the companion object to enable serialization of DynamicSchema instances. This is mentioned as "Part 6: Schema[DynamicSchema] (Hand-written)" in the issue. However, this schema definition is missing from the implementation. Without this, DynamicSchema cannot be serialized/deserialized, which is a core requirement for schema registries and wire format negotiation mentioned in the issue.
```suggestion
  }

  /**
   * Schema for serializing and deserializing [[DynamicSchema]] values.
   *
   * This is defined in terms of the underlying [[Reflect.Unbound]] schema,
   * since a [[DynamicSchema]] is just a thin wrapper around it.
   */
  implicit val schema: Schema[DynamicSchema] =
    Schema[Reflect.Unbound[_]].transform(
      reflect => DynamicSchema(reflect),
      _.reflect
    )
```

author: Copilot
body: There's no test coverage for the `Reflect.Wrapper` case in validation. The checkValue method handles wrappers (line 239-240), but there are no tests verifying that wrapped values are validated correctly. author: Copilot
body: The `defaultValue` and `examples` setter methods don't handle `Reflect.Deferred` cases. When the reflect is a Deferred, these methods will fall through to the `case other` branch which returns the reflect unchanged, silently ignoring the attempt to set a default value or examples. This is inconsistent with how the getter methods (`getDefaultValue` and `examples`) handle Deferred by recursively resolving them. Consider adding explicit handling for Deferred cases in the setter methods, or document this behavior if it's intentional.

author: Copilot
body: Similar to the `defaultValue` method, the `examples` setter method doesn't handle `Reflect.Deferred` cases. When the reflect is a Deferred, this method will fall through to the `case other` branch which returns the reflect unchanged, silently ignoring the attempt to set examples. This is inconsistent with how the getter method handles Deferred by recursively resolving them. Consider adding explicit handling for Deferred cases, or document this behavior if it's intentional.

author: Copilot
body:
The `toJsonSchema` implementation doesn't derive from `DynamicSchema`'s structure; it derives from `Schema[DynamicValue]` which represents *any* `DynamicValue`. This means all `DynamicSchema` instances will produce the same generic JSON schema, rather than a JSON schema specific to their structure.

Consider instead deriving from the unbound reflect: you likely need to create a `Schema[DynamicValue]` wrapper around the `DynamicSchema` (using `toSchema`) and then call `toJsonSchema` on that, or implement a direct JSON schema derivation from the unbound reflect structure.
```suggestion
  def toJsonSchema: JsonSchema = toSchema.toJsonSchema
```

Mismatch between field name in hand-written schema and serialization logic. The schema at line 716 uses "flatten" as the field name, but the serialization logic at line 1612 uses "leaves". This inconsistency will cause deserialization to fail.

The field should be named "flatten" in both places to match the actual Doc.Concat case class field name.
```suggestion
      case Doc.Concat(flatten) =>
```

author: Copilot
body:
Critical mismatch between PrimitiveType schema structure and serialization logic. The hand-written schema defines PrimitiveType as a Variant with cases "Unit", "Boolean", "Byte", etc. (lines 1287-1317), where each case contains a "validation" field. However, the serialization function `primitiveTypeToDV` (lines 1685-1693) incorrectly serializes it as a Record with "type" and "validation" fields.

The serialization should produce a Variant matching the schema structure, for example:
- For PrimitiveType.Int(validation) it should produce: DynamicValue.Variant("Int", DynamicValue.Record(Chunk("validation" -> validationToDV(validation))))
- For PrimitiveType.Unit it should produce: DynamicValue.Variant("Unit", DynamicValue.Record(Chunk.empty))
```suggestion
      val caseName = pt.typeName.name
      val payload =
        if (caseName == "Unit")
          DynamicValue.Record(Chunk.empty)
        else
          DynamicValue.Record(
            Chunk(
              "validation" -> validationToDV(pt.validation)
            )
          )
      DynamicValue.Variant(caseName, payload)
```

Missing comprehensive test coverage for PrimitiveType and Validation serialization through DynamicSchema. The existing tests in ValidationSpec and PrimitiveTypeSpec only test direct Schema[Validation[_]] and Schema[PrimitiveType[_]] round-trips, but don't test these types being embedded within a DynamicSchema and serialized through Schema[DynamicSchema].

This gap would have caught the critical bugs in primitiveTypeToDV and dvToPrimitiveType (comments ID 002 and 003). Consider adding tests that:

2. Convert it to DynamicSchema
3. Serialize via Schema[DynamicSchema].toDynamicValue
4. Deserialize and verify the validation is preserved

author: Copilot
body:
`dvToPrimitiveType` reconstructs a `PrimitiveType` from a `DynamicValue` using only the `type` field and hard-codes `Validation.None` for all non-`Unit` cases, ignoring the serialized `validation` data. This means that when a `DynamicSchema` is round-tripped via `Schema[DynamicSchema]`/`DynamicSchema.fromDynamicValue`, any numeric or string validations (e.g., ranges, sets, non-empty) attached to primitive fields are silently dropped, so subsequent `DynamicSchema.check` calls will accept inputs that should have been rejected. To preserve data integrity and avoid validation bypasses in consumers that persist and reload schemas, `dvToPrimitiveType` should deserialize and re-attach the correct `Validation[_]` from the `primitiveType` record instead of defaulting to `Validation.None`.
```suggestion
        // Extract validation information if present; otherwise default to Validation.None
        val validationAny: Validation[Any] =
          fieldMap
            .get("validation")
            .map(dvToValidation)
            .getOrElse(Validation.None)
            .asInstanceOf[Validation[Any]]
        typeName match {
          case "Unit" =>
            PrimitiveType.Unit.asInstanceOf[PrimitiveType[Any]]
          case "Boolean" =>
            PrimitiveType.Boolean(validationAny.asInstanceOf[Validation[Boolean]]).asInstanceOf[PrimitiveType[Any]]
          case "Byte" =>
            PrimitiveType.Byte(validationAny.asInstanceOf[Validation[Byte]]).asInstanceOf[PrimitiveType[Any]]
          case "Short" =>
            PrimitiveType.Short(validationAny.asInstanceOf[Validation[Short]]).asInstanceOf[PrimitiveType[Any]]
          case "Int" =>
            PrimitiveType.Int(validationAny.asInstanceOf[Validation[Int]]).asInstanceOf[PrimitiveType[Any]]
          case "Long" =>
            PrimitiveType.Long(validationAny.asInstanceOf[Validation[Long]]).asInstanceOf[PrimitiveType[Any]]
          case "Float" =>
            PrimitiveType.Float(validationAny.asInstanceOf[Validation[Float]]).asInstanceOf[PrimitiveType[Any]]
          case "Double" =>
            PrimitiveType.Double(validationAny.asInstanceOf[Validation[Double]]).asInstanceOf[PrimitiveType[Any]]
          case "Char" =>
            PrimitiveType.Char(validationAny.asInstanceOf[Validation[Char]]).asInstanceOf[PrimitiveType[Any]]
          case "String" =>
            PrimitiveType.String(validationAny.asInstanceOf[Validation[String]]).asInstanceOf[PrimitiveType[Any]]
          case "BigInt" =>
            PrimitiveType.BigInt(validationAny.asInstanceOf[Validation[BigInt]]).asInstanceOf[PrimitiveType[Any]]
          case "BigDecimal" =>
            PrimitiveType.BigDecimal(validationAny.asInstanceOf[Validation[BigDecimal]]).asInstanceOf[PrimitiveType[Any]]
          case "UUID" =>
            PrimitiveType.UUID(validationAny.asInstanceOf[Validation[java.util.UUID]]).asInstanceOf[PrimitiveType[Any]]
          case "Instant" =>
            PrimitiveType.Instant(validationAny.asInstanceOf[Validation[java.time.Instant]]).asInstanceOf[PrimitiveType[Any]]
          case "LocalDate" =>
            PrimitiveType.LocalDate(validationAny.asInstanceOf[Validation[java.time.LocalDate]]).asInstanceOf[PrimitiveType[Any]]
          case "LocalDateTime" =>
            PrimitiveType.LocalDateTime(validationAny.asInstanceOf[Validation[java.time.LocalDateTime]]).asInstanceOf[PrimitiveType[Any]]
          case "LocalTime" =>
            PrimitiveType.LocalTime(validationAny.asInstanceOf[Validation[java.time.LocalTime]]).asInstanceOf[PrimitiveType[Any]]
          case "Duration" =>
            PrimitiveType.Duration(validationAny.asInstanceOf[Validation[java.time.Duration]]).asInstanceOf[PrimitiveType[Any]]
          case _ =>
            PrimitiveType.String(validationAny.asInstanceOf[Validation[String]]).asInstanceOf[PrimitiveType[Any]]
        }
      case _ =>
        PrimitiveType.String(Validation.None).asInstanceOf[PrimitiveType[Any]]
```

author: Copilot
body:
The manual serialization of `Validation.Numeric.Range` uses `DynamicValue.Null` for None values, but the schema at line 889 defines the fields as `Schema[Option[String]]`. This creates an inconsistency because `Schema[Option[String]]` serializes None as a `DynamicValue.Variant("None", ...)`, not as `DynamicValue.Null`.

This inconsistency means that round-trip serialization via `DynamicSchema.toDynamicValue` and `DynamicSchema.fromDynamicValue` may not match the round-trip serialization via the `validationRangeSchema`. Consider using the schema's serialization approach consistently, or using the Option schema's representation (Variant with "None" and "Some" cases) in the manual serialization.
```suggestion
                .map(v =>
                  DynamicValue.Variant(
                    "Some",
                    DynamicValue.Record(
                      Chunk(
                        "value" -> DynamicValue.Primitive(PrimitiveValue.String(v.toString))
                      )
                    )
                  )
                )
                .getOrElse(DynamicValue.Variant("None", DynamicValue.Record(Chunk.empty))),
              "max" -> max
                .map(v =>
                  DynamicValue.Variant(
                    "Some",
                    DynamicValue.Record(
                      Chunk(
                        "value" -> DynamicValue.Primitive(PrimitiveValue.String(v.toString))
                      )
                    )
                  )
                )
                .getOrElse(DynamicValue.Variant("None", DynamicValue.Record(Chunk.empty)))
```

Consider using the Option schema's representation consistently throughout.

author: Copilot
body:
`Lazy(null.asInstanceOf[TC[A0]])` is used to build a temporary `Reflect.Record` for `fromDynamicValue` conversion. If this `Lazy` is ever forced (now or via future refactors), it will fail with an unhelpful NPE. Prefer a failing lazy value (e.g., `Lazy.fail(new IllegalStateException("...") )`) so accidental evaluation is diagnosed clearly, or restructure to avoid needing a dummy instance.
```suggestion
                new BindingInstance(
                  metadata,
                  Lazy.fail(
                    new IllegalStateException(
                      "Temporary type class instance for fromDynamicValue must not be evaluated"
                    )
                  )
                ),
```

body:
The `sizeHint` implementation has a potential bug. When `array` is null, it creates a new array using `Chunk.Tags.fromValue(null.asInstanceOf[A])`, which will fail to get a proper ClassTag for the array type. This should only set the capacity hint without creating the array, or handle the null case differently.

The original implementation correctly deferred array creation until the first element was added (which provides the type information), but the new implementation tries to create the array in `sizeHint` before any element is available.

author: Copilot
body:
The `knownSize` implementation is incorrect. It returns `array.length` instead of `count` (the actual number of elements added). The array length is the capacity, not the size. This will return incorrect values when the array has been resized or when `sizeHint` was used.
```suggestion
      else count
```

author: Copilot
body: Using `left.until(end)` instead of `first until end` changes the semantics. The variable `first` contains the start value, not `left`. This appears to be a copy-paste error that will produce incorrect range values.

author: Copilot
body:
The loop logic is inverted. The condition `idx < len && !f(iterator.nextAt(idx))` should be `idx < len && f(iterator.nextAt(idx))` to properly drop elements while the predicate is true. This will cause `dropWhile` to drop until the first element that matches the predicate instead of dropping while the predicate matches.
```suggestion
    while (idx < len && f(iterator.nextAt(idx))) idx += 1
```

body:
The return value changed from `take(i)` to `Chunk.empty` at the end of the while loop, but this is incorrect. When the loop completes without finding a non-matching element, it means all elements match the predicate, so the function should return `self` (the entire chunk), not an empty chunk.
```suggestion
    self
```

author: Copilot
body:
According to the PR description and linked issue #869, the `Binding.Seq` and `Binding.Map` types should be made covariant in their element type parameters. The issue states: "After this refactor, A becomes covariant in Binding.Seq[C, +A] and Binding.Map[M, +K, +V]." However, the actual implementation is missing the covariance markers. The case classes should be:
- `final case class Seq[C[_], +A]` instead of `final case class Seq[C[_], A]`
- `final case class Map[M[_, _], +K, +V]` instead of `final case class Map[M[_, _], K, V]`

This covariance is now possible because `defaultValue` and `examples` fields (which were contravariant in their usage) have been removed from these classes.

This covariance is now possible because `defaultValue` and `examples` fields (which were contravariant in their usage) have been removed from this class.

author: Copilot
body:
The Scaladoc reference has been changed from `DynamicOptic` to `DynamicPatch`, but this is semantically incorrect. The comment states "for path-based navigation", which is what `DynamicOptic` provides, not `DynamicPatch`. 

`DynamicOptic` is a path-based lens system for navigating and modifying nested structures (as stated on line 24 of this same file). `DynamicPatch`, on the other hand, is for representing and applying patches/diffs to DynamicValues.

The correct fix should be to use the fully qualified reference `[[zio.blocks.schema.DynamicOptic]]` instead of `[[zio.blocks.schema.patch.DynamicPatch]]` to resolve the Scaladoc compilation issue while maintaining the correct semantic meaning.
```suggestion
 *   [[zio.blocks.schema.DynamicOptic]] for path-based navigation
```

In `flatMap`, `results.collect { case Right(v) => builder.addAll(v) }` allocates a new `Vector` just to run side effects on `builder`, which defeats the intended allocation/perf optimization. Iterate with `foreach`/`while` (or `builder ++= v`) instead so no intermediate collection is built.

author: Copilot
body:
`Json.Number(BigInt)` constructs a `BigDecimal` using `BigDecimal.defaultMathContext`, which can round large integers and lose precision. Use an exact conversion (no `MathContext`) so the `BigInt` value is preserved exactly.
```suggestion
    def apply(value: BigInt): Number = new Number(BigDecimal(value))
```

author: Copilot
body: The new numeric decoder error message drops the offending number value (previously included in the message), which makes debugging harder. Consider including the value (possibly truncated) in the error, e.g. "Number <value> is not a valid Int".

author: Copilot
body: `fromKVUnsafe` starts from `Record.empty` and repeatedly calls `result = result.insert(path, value)`, but `insertAtPath` returns `None` when the parent containers for a path do not already exist, so deeply nested paths (e.g. `.field("a").field("b")`) will be silently ignored instead of constructing intermediate records/sequences/maps. Given the "KV Reconstruction" comment and the behavior of `toKV` (which only emits leaf paths), callers would reasonably expect `fromKV` to be able to reconstruct arbitrary nested structures from leaf paths alone. To make `fromKV` usable, `insertAtPath` (or a dedicated reconstruction helper) should create any missing parent containers along the path rather than treating them as an insertion failure.

author: Copilot
body:
In the Avro `dynamicValueCodec`, encoding `DynamicValue.Null` writes a top-level union tag `0` followed by an inner index `0`, which is exactly the same pattern used for `PrimitiveValue.Unit` (primitive tag 0, enum index 0) in `decodeUnsafe`. As a result, Avro round-tripping cannot distinguish `DynamicValue.Null` from a primitive Unit: any encoded `DynamicValue.Null` will be decoded as `DynamicValue.Primitive(PrimitiveValue.Unit)`. To preserve the new `Null` case, the Avro schema/union and decoder need a distinct tag (or additional discriminator) for `Null` and a corresponding branch in `decodeUnsafe` so that `Null` encodes and decodes symmetrically.
```suggestion
              // Use a distinct top-level tag for Null so it does not collide with PrimitiveValue.Unit.
              encoder.writeInt(5)
```

author: Copilot
body:
The new `DynamicValueMergeStrategy.Custom` default `r` predicate is documented as "always recurse for containers" but it only returns true for `Record`, `Sequence`, and `Map`, omitting `Variant` even though variants are treated as containers elsewhere (e.g., in `Auto.recurse` and `mergeImpl`). This discrepancy means that the default `Custom` strategy does not recursively merge variants as implied by the Scaladoc, which can be surprising for users expecting consistent container handling. Either the documentation should be updated to call out the difference for variants, or the default `r` should include `DynamicValueType.Variant` to match the described behavior.
```suggestion
      t == DynamicValueType.Record ||
        t == DynamicValueType.Sequence ||
        t == DynamicValueType.Map ||
        t == DynamicValueType.Variant
```

body:
`Differ.diff` already returns `DynamicPatch.empty` when `oldValue == newValue`, so the explicit `(DynamicValue.Null, DynamicValue.Null)` case added here is unreachable and will never be exercised (equality on `Null` is true, so the match is skipped). This dead branch is misleading and suggests special-case handling that does not actually occur at runtime; removing it would simplify the diff logic and avoid confusion. If you intend to handle additional `Null`-related cases, they need to be expressed in branches that are reachable after the initial equality check.
```suggestion

```

author: Copilot
body:
In `modifyAtPathImpl`, the `Elements` case drops sequence elements whose nested path cannot be modified, instead of leaving them unchanged. For example, modifying `root.elements.field("x")` on a sequence where only some elements have a record field `x` will filter out all non-matching elements, shrinking the sequence, which is inconsistent with how the other node cases work and with the documented behavior of `modify` ("returns the original DynamicValue unchanged" when the path doesn't exist). You likely want to preserve all original elements and only update the ones where the nested path is found, e.g. by mapping over `s.elements` and falling back to the original element when the recursive call returns `None`, rather than using `flatMap` and treating `None` as element removal.
```suggestion
            var found = false
            val newElems = s.elements.map { e =>
              modifyAtPathImpl(e, nodes, idx + 1, f) match {
                case Some(nv) =>
                  found = true
                  nv
                case None => e
              }
            }
            if (found) Some(Sequence(newElems)) else None
```

author: Copilot
body:
In `modifyAtPathImpl`, both the `MapKeys` and `MapValues` cases use `flatMap` over the recursive `modifyAtPathImpl` result and drop entries where the nested path cannot be modified, which effectively removes key/value pairs from the map when no change should be applied. This differs from the behavior of `Field`, `AtMapKey`, and `AtIndices`, which preserve unchanged children, and it contradicts the contract of `modify`/`modifyOrFail` that a missing path leaves the original structure intact. To align semantics, these branches should keep entries whose recursive modification returns `None` (reusing the original key or value) and only replace entries where a `Some` result is returned, rather than filtering entries out.
```suggestion
            var found      = false
            val newEntries = m.entries.map { case (k, v) =>
              modifyAtPathImpl(k, nodes, idx + 1, f) match {
                case Some(nk) =>
                  found = true
                  (nk, v)
                case None => (k, v)
              }
            }
            if (found) Some(Map(newEntries)) else None
          case _ => None
        }

      case DynamicOptic.Node.MapValues =>
        dv match {
          case m: Map =>
            var found      = false
            val newEntries = m.entries.map { case (k, v) =>
              modifyAtPathImpl(v, nodes, idx + 1, f) match {
                case Some(nv) =>
                  found = true
                  (k, nv)
                case None => (k, v)
              }
            }
            if (found) Some(Map(newEntries)) else None
```

author: Copilot
body: `flatMap` currently fails fast on the first `Left` and discards any successfully collected values, while the PR description advertises multi-value selections with error accumulation; this behavior means you never get partial results with a combined error and it does not accumulate multiple `DynamicValueError`s. If the intended behavior is true error accumulation as documented, consider collecting all errors (e.g., via a non-empty collection of `DynamicValueError` or by combining them with `DynamicValueError.++`) and only returning a failure when at least one error is present, or updating the public documentation to match the actual fail-fast semantics.

author: Copilot
body: The new `DynamicValueSelection` API introduces a rich set of behaviors (navigation, combinators, query methods, type-directed extraction, etc.) but there are no dedicated tests for it, whereas the analogous `JsonSelection` has comprehensive coverage (see `schema/shared/src/test/scala/zio/blocks/schema/json/JsonSelectionSpec.scala`). To reduce regression risk for this DSL, consider adding a `DynamicValueSelectionSpec` that mirrors the key scenarios covered for `JsonSelection` (e.g., `map`/`flatMap`, `one`/`any`/`all`, type filters, `query*`, and error propagation).

author: Copilot
body: `dynamicValueCodec.decodeValue` still maps JSON `null` to `unitValue` (`DynamicValue.Primitive(PrimitiveValue.Unit)`), while `encodeValue` now has a dedicated `case DynamicValue.Null => out.writeNull()`. That means `decodeValue(encodeValue(DynamicValue.Null))` will not round-trip back to `DynamicValue.Null` but to a `Primitive(Unit)`, which is inconsistent with the new `DynamicValue.Null` semantics and with `Json.toDynamicValue`/`fromDynamicValue`. Consider updating `decodeValue` so that `null` tokens produce `DynamicValue.Null` (and only treat `PrimitiveValue.Unit` as `null` where you intentionally want that legacy behavior), or at least ensure round-trips preserve `Null` vs `Unit` distinctly where supported.

author: Copilot
body:
In `pruneImpl`, the `Variant` case does not actually prune matching nested values and instead returns the original `Variant` unchanged when `p(childPath, v.value)` is true:

- For `Record`, `Sequence`, and `Map`, children for which `p` returns true are removed from the container.
- For `Variant`, if `p(childPath, v.value)` is true, the function returns `dv` without recursing, so values that should be pruned remain, and the predicate effectively *disables* pruning beneath variants.

This contradicts the documented behavior of `prune` ("recursively removes elements/fields for which the predicate returns true") and makes pruning inconsistent for values wrapped in variants. A more consistent implementation would mirror the object/sequence/map branches: either (a) treat the `Variant` as an atomic value and never call `p` on `v.value` here (just recurse with `Variant(v.caseNameValue, pruneImpl(v.value, childPath, p))`), or (b) if you want to allow pruning by the inner value, have `p` control removal of the variant at the *parent* container level, not by short-circuiting to `dv` in this branch.
```suggestion
      Variant(v.caseNameValue, pruneImpl(v.value, childPath, p))
```

author: Copilot
body: `processEscapes` is applied to `StringContext` parts, which can corrupt valid JSON escape sequences. JSON text containing backslash escapes (e.g. `\\n`, `\\"`, `\\\\`) must keep those backslashes for the JSON parser, but `processEscapes` converts them into raw control characters/quotes, potentially making the JSON invalid or changing its meaning. The compiler already provides the correct string literal values here; please remove this extra escape processing.

author: Copilot
body:
`processEscapes` is applied to `StringContext` parts, which can corrupt valid JSON escape sequences (e.g. `\\n`, `\\"`, `\\\\`) by turning them into raw control characters/quotes before JSON parsing. Since the compiler already gives the correct string literal values, this extra unescaping should be removed to avoid breaking JSON literals that rely on backslash escapes.
```suggestion
          case Literal(Constant(part: String)) => part
```

body: `isKeyPosition(cumulativeText.toString, nextPart)` allocates a full `String` (and then `trim`s it) on every interpolation. Since only the last non-whitespace char before the hole and the first non-whitespace char after are needed, consider avoiding `toString/trim` inside the loop (scan `cumulativeText` backwards and `nextPart` forwards) to prevent O(n^2) behavior for larger templates.

author: Copilot
body: In this PrettyPrinter example, `binding.deconstructor.projectDynamic` is used to access record fields, but `Binding.Record`’s `deconstructor` is a `Deconstructor[A]` that does not define `projectDynamic`, and there is no `projectDynamic` method anywhere in the codebase. As written, this snippet will not compile under `mdoc`. Please update the example to use the actual binding/reflect APIs for accessing field values (e.g., via the `Term`/`Reflect` abstractions) so that the code matches the real API surface.

author: Copilot
body:
The variant derivation in this PrettyPrinter example calls `binding.deconstructor.indexOf(value)` and `binding.deconstructor.projectDynamic(value, caseIndex)`, but `Binding.Variant` does not have a `deconstructor` member and there are no `indexOf`/`projectDynamic` methods exposed on any binding deconstructor types. This code cannot compile against the current `Binding.Variant` API and will fail `mdoc` compilation; consider rewriting the example to use the existing `Discriminator`/`Matchers` machinery (or higher-level `Reflect` APIs) to select the active case and its value.
```suggestion
          val discriminator = binding.discriminator
          val matchers      = binding.matchers
          val caseIndex     = discriminator.indexOf(value)
          val selectedCase  = cases(caseIndex)
          val caseValue     = matchers.project(value, caseIndex)
          val printer       = D.instance(selectedCase.reflect).value
```

author: Copilot
body:
For sequences, this example relies on `binding.deconstructor.toChunk(value)`, but `Binding.Seq`’s `deconstructor` is a `SeqDeconstructor[C]` which exposes `deconstruct`/`size` (and, for specialized variants, element accessors) rather than a `toChunk` method; there is no `toChunk` on `SeqDeconstructor` in the codebase. This will not compile as-is; please adjust the example to use the real `SeqDeconstructor` API (e.g., iterating via `deconstruct` and materializing a collection) so it reflects how custom derivers should be implemented.
```suggestion
          val printer       = D.instance(element).value
          val deconstructor = binding.deconstructor
          val buffer        = scala.collection.mutable.ArrayBuffer.empty[A]
          deconstructor.deconstruct(value, (a: A) => buffer += a)
          val elements      = buffer.toVector
```

This mismatch means the snippet will not compile and misleads users about the available API; consider rewriting it to iterate using `MapDeconstructor.deconstruct` (or its `getKey`/`getValue` helpers) instead.
```suggestion
          val deconstructed = binding.deconstructor.deconstruct(value)
          val size         = binding.deconstructor.size(deconstructed)
          val entryStrings = (0 until size).map { i =>
            val k = binding.deconstructor.getKey(deconstructed, i)
            val v = binding.deconstructor.getValue(deconstructed, i)
```

author: Copilot
body:
The `Format` trait shown here does not match the actual implementation in `schema/shared/src/main/scala/zio/blocks/schema/codec/Format.scala`: in the real code, `TypeClass[A]` is a subtype of `Codec[DecodeInput, EncodeOutput, A]`, not just `Codec[A]`, and `DecodeInput`/`EncodeOutput` are the type parameters of that codec. Please update this snippet so the type members and `TypeClass` alias line up with the real `Format` definition, otherwise users attempting to implement custom formats from this example will hit type errors.
```suggestion
  type DecodeInput
  type EncodeOutput
  type TypeClass[A] <: Codec[DecodeInput, EncodeOutput, A]
```

body:
In the caching example, the same `ByteBuffer` is passed to `Schema[Person].decode(JsonFormat)(buffer)` immediately after encoding without flipping or rewinding it, unlike the earlier `Schema` reference docs which call `buffer.flip()` before decode. Given how `BinaryCodec` implementations work with `ByteBuffer`, this pattern is unlikely to decode correctly and can confuse users; consider adding a flip/rewind (or using a fresh buffer/view) here so the sample is consistent with the recommended usage elsewhere.
```suggestion
Schema[Person].encode(JsonFormat)(buffer)(person)
buffer.flip()
```

author: Copilot

body:
The hex encoding for control characters in `writeEscapedStringFragment` is incorrect. When writing the 2-digit hex representation, it only writes the last character of the hex string instead of writing all hex digits properly.

For control characters like `\^A` (hex "1"), this code writes `\u001`, which is incomplete. For `\^P` (hex "10"), it writes `\^P` but only by accident (writing the last char '0').

The correct implementation should write both hex digits:
```scala
val hex = Integer.toHexString(c.toInt)
if (hex.length == 1) {
  out.write('0')
  out.write(hex.charAt(0))
} else {
  out.write(hex.charAt(0))
  out.write(hex.charAt(1))
}
```

Or more simply:
```scala
val hex = Integer.toHexString(c.toInt)
val padded = if (hex.length == 1) "0" + hex else hex
out.write(padded.charAt(0))
out.write(padded.charAt(1))
```

body: The removal of `TypeName.derived` and the files `TypeNameCompanionVersionSpecific.scala` represents a breaking API change. Users who were calling `TypeName.derived[SomeType]` will now get compilation errors. This should be clearly documented in the PR description or in a CHANGELOG/migration guide, explaining what users should do instead (likely they should rely on implicit TypeName resolution or Schema derivation).

author: Copilot
body: The `DerivedOptics` trait API has changed significantly. In Scala 3, the trait previously supported both direct field access (e.g., `Person.name`) and explicit `.optics` access. Now it only supports `.optics` access and requires a type parameter. This is a breaking change for existing users. The change from `DerivedOptics.Of[T]` to `DerivedOptics[T]` should be documented in migration notes.

author: Copilot
body: The documentation shows `wrap` with signature `wrap: B => Either[String, A]` (returning `Left("Invalid email format")`), but the actual implementation in Schema.scala has signature `wrap: B => Either[SchemaError, A]`. This is a discrepancy between the documentation and the implementation. The error type should be `SchemaError` not `String`, and users would need to use `SchemaError.validationFailed("Invalid email format")` or similar.

author: Copilot
body: The CI workflow has removed the concurrency control that was previously present. This means that if multiple PR builds or pushes happen simultaneously, they won't cancel previous in-progress builds. This could lead to wasted CI resources and longer wait times for PR feedback. The concurrency control is a best practice for CI efficiency.

author: Copilot
body: All coverage thresholds have been removed from the build configuration (both minimum statement and branch coverage targets). This means the build will no longer enforce any minimum code coverage standards. While this may simplify the build process, it reduces quality gates and could allow coverage to regress over time without detection. Consider whether this is intentional or if coverage metrics should still be tracked.

```yaml
  cancel-in-progress: true
```

author: Copilot
body:
The `writeEscapedStringFragment` method writes characters using `out.write(other.toString)` for non-control characters. This could be problematic for characters outside the Basic Multilingual Plane (BMP) that are represented as surrogate pairs in UTF-16. The `toString` method on a `Char` will not properly handle the second half of a surrogate pair, potentially producing invalid output.

Consider using a more robust approach that handles surrogate pairs correctly, similar to how the JsonBinaryCodec handles this elsewhere in the codebase.

body:
The merge pattern match does not explicitly handle MergeStrategy.Deep and MergeStrategy.Concat. These case objects still exist in MergeStrategy.scala but fall through to the default case (line 788), which implements the same behavior as MergeStrategy.Auto.

While the tests pass because Deep and Concat happen to have the same behavior as Auto for the tested scenarios, this design may be confusing:
- If Deep and Concat are meant to be removed or deprecated, they should be deleted from MergeStrategy.scala
- If they are meant to have distinct behavior, they should be explicitly handled in this pattern match
- If they are intentionally aliased to Auto, this should be documented

Consider either:
1. Removing MergeStrategy.Deep and MergeStrategy.Concat entirely from MergeStrategy.scala, OR
2. Adding explicit cases for them with comments explaining they behave the same as Auto, OR
3. Adding a deprecation annotation if they're being phased out

The `partition` method signature on line 425 uses the old two-parameter predicate `(_, v) => v.is(JsonType.Number)`. According to the PR description and test changes, `partition` now takes a single parameter predicate on the value. The correct usage should be:

```scala
val (numbers, nonNumbers) = json.partition(_.is(JsonType.Number))
```

If both path and value are needed, use `partitionBoth`:
```scala
val (numbers, nonNumbers) = json.partitionBoth((_, v) => v.is(JsonType.Number))
```

```suggestion
val (numbers, nonNumbers) = json.partition(_.is(JsonType.Number))
```

author: Copilot
body:
The `mergeByIndex` function uses `Chunk.from` with a `Range.map` operation that creates an intermediate collection. This could be inefficient for large arrays. Consider using `ChunkBuilder` for better performance:

```scala
val builder = ChunkBuilder.make[Json]()
var i = 0
while (i < maxLen) {
  val childPath = path.at(i)
  (left.value.lift(i), right.value.lift(i)) match {
    case (Some(lv), Some(rv)) => builder += mergeImpl(childPath, lv, rv, s)
    case (Some(lv), None)     => builder += lv
    case (None, Some(rv))     => builder += rv
    case (None, None)         => throw new IllegalStateException("...")
  }
  i += 1
}
new Array(builder.result())
```
```suggestion
    val maxLen  = Math.max(left.value.length, right.value.length)
    val builder = ChunkBuilder.make[Json]()
    var i       = 0
    while (i < maxLen) {
      val childPath = path.at(i)
      (left.value.lift(i), right.value.lift(i)) match {
        case (Some(lv), Some(rv)) => builder += mergeImpl(childPath, lv, rv, s)
        case (Some(lv), None)     => builder += lv
        case (None, Some(rv))     => builder += rv
        case (None, None)         => throw new IllegalStateException("Index should exist in at least one array")
      }
      i += 1
    }
    new Array(builder.result())
```

body:
The context analysis is incorrect for string literal detection. The `isInsideString` function is called on each part independently starting with `inString = false`, but it should carry forward the state from previous parts.

For example, with `json"""{"start$x middle $y end"}"""`, the parts are `["{"start", " middle ", " end"}"]`. For interpolation 1 (between parts 1 and 2), `isInsideString(" middle ")` returns false because it starts fresh without knowing we're already inside a string from the previous parts. The correct context should be "string" but it would be misidentified as "value".

The analyzeContexts function needs to accumulate state across parts, similar to how the runtime uses `updateStringState` incrementally.

Missing test coverage for multiple interpolations within the same JSON string literal. The test at line 628-635 has multiple interpolations but in different parts of the JSON structure (different string values). There should be a test like:

`json"""{"msg": "Hello $firstName $lastName"}"""`

author: Copilot
body:
Missing test coverage for non-stringable type in a subsequent (non-first) interpolation within the same JSON string literal. For example:

`json"""{"msg": "Value $stringableVar and $nonStringableVar"}"""`

This would expose the bug in analyzeContexts where only the first interpolation in a string is correctly identified as "string" context, while subsequent ones are misidentified as "value" context. This allows non-stringable types to bypass compile-time validation for the second and later interpolations within the same string.

body: The Native macro implementation does not validate JSON syntax at compile time, while the JVM/JS implementations do (see lines 27-40 in schema/js-jvm/src/main/scala-2/zio/blocks/schema/json/package.scala). This inconsistency means that invalid JSON literals will fail at compile time on JVM/JS but only at runtime on Native, leading to a platform-dependent developer experience. Consider adding the same validation to the Native implementation for consistency.

author: Copilot
body: The context detection has O(n²) time complexity where n is the number of interpolations. On line 29, `detectContextFromBeforeAfter` is called with `accumulatedText.toString` which converts the StringBuilder to a String, and then `isInStringLiteral` scans this entire accumulated text. This happens for each interpolation that's not inside a string literal. While this is mitigated by the string literal tracking optimization, consider caching the result of `isInStringLiteral` for the accumulated text or restructuring to avoid repeated scans of the same prefix.

body:
The compile-time error message lists path syntax like `[<index>]`, `{*}`, and `.~`, but the `CompanionOptics.optic` macro actually expects Scala method syntax such as `.at(i)`, `.each`, `.eachKey`, `.eachValue`, `.atKey(k)`, `.wrapped[T]`, and `.when[T]`. As written, the message suggests non-Scala syntax that users cannot type, making macro errors harder to resolve. Update the message to reflect the actual supported DSL (optionally mentioning the `DynamicOptic.toString` representation separately).
```suggestion
                s"Invalid optic path. Expected elements built using the optic DSL (e.g. .field, <<T>>, .wrapped[T], .when[T], .at(index), .each, .eachKey, .eachValue, .atKey(key)). " +
                  s"(Internal representation looks like .<field>, <<T>>, [<index>], [<indices>], {<key>}, {<keys>}, [*], {*:}, {*}, or .~.) Got '${term.show}'."
```

author: Copilot
body:
`escapeString` wraps the raw text in quotes without escaping (e.g. embedded `"`, `\n`, `\\`). This can produce invalid / misleading patch output for `StringOp.Insert/Append/Modify` when the inserted text contains quotes, newlines, or backslashes. Consider reusing the same JSON/EJSON string escaping logic used by `DynamicValue` / `Json` so patch rendering is always unambiguous.
```suggestion
  private def escapeString(s: String): String = {
    val sb = new StringBuilder("\"")
    s.foreach {
      case '"'  => sb.append("\\\"")
      case '\\' => sb.append("\\\\")
      case '\b' => sb.append("\\b")
      case '\f' => sb.append("\\f")
      case '\n' => sb.append("\\n")
      case '\r' => sb.append("\\r")
      case '\t' => sb.append("\\t")
      case c if c < ' ' =>
        sb.append(f"\\u${c.toInt}%04x")
      case c =>
        sb.append(c)
    }
    sb.append("\"")
    sb.toString
  }
```

body: This `PosInt` is an `AnyVal` wrapper (runtime representation is the underlying `Int`). Using `withTypeName` here leaves `wrapperPrimitiveType = None` (see `Schema.transformOrFail`), so the schema loses the primitive-register optimization that `asOpaqueType` is meant to enable. If the goal is optimized storage for newtypes/value classes, this should likely use `.asOpaqueType[PosInt]` instead of `.withTypeName[PosInt]` (matching the PR description).

body: `PosInt` is an `AnyVal` wrapper, so its runtime representation is the underlying `Int`. With the new API, `.withTypeName` keeps `wrapperPrimitiveType = None`, which disables the opaque/newtype primitive optimization. If this test is meant to cover the optimized path, consider switching to `.asOpaqueType[PosInt]` instead of `.withTypeName[PosInt]`.

author: Copilot
body: `withTypeName` is type-unsafe: it allows changing the schema’s type parameter from `A` to an unrelated `B` (via `asInstanceOf`), even though the underlying `reflect` still describes `A`. This can create a `Schema[B]` that encodes/decodes as `A` with only the name changed. Consider restricting this to the current schema type (e.g., `def withTypeName(implicit tn: TypeName[A]): Schema[A]`) or requiring evidence `B =:= A` to keep the API sound.

author: Copilot
body:
With the current `concurrency.group`, all matrix permutations for this job share a single concurrency slot. That will serialize the matrix and may cancel/queue work in ways that significantly slow CI.

Prefer workflow-level concurrency for “cancel previous PR runs”, or make the group unique per matrix entry by including relevant `matrix.*` values.

author: Copilot
body:
The `concurrency.group` value is identical across multiple jobs, so only one job (and only one matrix permutation) can run at a time for a given PR/ref. This will serialize `lint` vs the test jobs and defeat matrix parallelism, and can also cause unrelated jobs from the previous run to be cancelled when a new run starts.

To cancel superseded PR runs without losing parallelism, prefer defining `concurrency` once at the workflow level, or (if you intentionally want job-level concurrency) include `github.job` and any `matrix.*` dimensions in the group key.

author: Copilot
body:
Because this job uses a matrix, the current `concurrency.group` value (which does not include any `matrix.*` fields) forces all matrix permutations to share one concurrency slot. That will serialize the matrix runs (and potentially cancel in-progress permutations from a previous run) rather than running them in parallel.

Consider moving `concurrency` to the workflow level to cancel older PR runs, or make the group key unique per matrix entry (e.g., include `matrix.java`/`matrix.scala`).

author: Copilot
body:
This job’s matrix permutations all share the same `concurrency.group`, which will prevent them from running in parallel and can lead to unexpected cancellations/queuing behavior.

To keep matrix parallelism while still cancelling superseded PR runs, use workflow-level concurrency or incorporate `matrix.*` dimensions into the group key.

body:
The `renderDynamicValueKey` function incorrectly quotes all non-string keys. According to the EJSON format specification in issue #802, only string keys in maps should be quoted. Non-string keys (like integers, booleans, etc.) should be rendered without quotes.

Currently, line 247-249 wraps all non-string keys in quotes:
```scala
case other =>
  sb.append('"')
  renderToStringBuilder(other, sb)
  sb.append('"')
```

This would render `{ 1: "one", 2: "two" }` as `{ "1": "one", "2": "two" }`, which is incorrect according to the spec.
```suggestion
      renderToStringBuilder(other, sb)
```

author: Copilot
body: The `&&` combinator creates nested allOf structures instead of flattening them. When combining two schemas that both have allOf, this produces `allOf: [allOf: [...], allOf: [...]]` instead of a single flattened list. The correct approach is to extract and merge the schemas from both allOf lists: `new ::(...)` should contain all schemas from both a1 and a2 flattened into a single list.

author: Copilot
body: The `||` combinator has the same issue as `&&` - it creates nested anyOf structures. When combining schemas with existing anyOf, all schemas should be flattened into a single anyOf list rather than creating nested structures.

`stripSuffix("$$")` likely won’t remove the trailing `$` from module/moduleClass names in Scala 2 reflection (they typically end with a single `$`). This can leak `$` into generated `Owner.Term(...)` segments and produce unstable/incorrect `fullName`s. Use `stripSuffix("$")` instead.
```suggestion
          val termName = s.name.decodedName.toString.stripSuffix("$")
```

author: Copilot
body: `structurallyEqual`/`structuralHash` only compare `fullName` and `typeParams`, ignoring `aliasedTo` contents and any applied type arguments. This makes distinct types collide (e.g., `TypeId.derived[List[Int]] == TypeId.derived[List[String]]`, and all synthetic unions named `Union` become equal). This will break Map/Set key usage and caches keyed by `TypeId`. Equality/hash need to incorporate the normalized type structure (including applied args / union & intersection members).

author: Copilot
body: `deriveUnionType` creates a `TypeId.alias` with a constant name/owner (`"Union"`, `Owner.Root`). Given current `TypeId` equality/hash semantics, this makes *all* union types (e.g. `Int | String` vs `Int | Double`) equal. The derived `TypeId` for unions/intersections needs a stable structural identity (e.g., include member `TypeRepr`s in equality/hash or encode members in the `TypeId` itself).

author: Copilot
body: `deriveAppliedType` ignores the actual type arguments and tries to reuse an existential `TypeId` for the type constructor (e.g., `List[_]`). Combined with the current `TypeId` equality/hash implementation, this makes `TypeId.derived[List[Int]]` collide with `TypeId.derived[List[String]]`. If `TypeId` is meant to uniquely identify applied types, derivation needs to capture the applied arguments (e.g., as part of the derived `TypeId` structure).

author: Copilot
body: `TypeDefKind.Object` is a case class (not a case object), so comparing with `defKind == TypeDefKind.Object` will always be false. Use `defKind.isInstanceOf[TypeDefKind.Object]` or pattern match instead so `isObject` can ever return true.

author: Copilot
body: Like the Scala 3 version, `structurallyEqual`/`structuralHash` only consider `fullName` and `typeParams` and ignore the actual applied type arguments and alias structure. This causes collisions (e.g., `TypeId.derived[List[Int]]` vs `TypeId.derived[List[String]]`) and makes `TypeId` unsafe as a Map/Set key. Equality/hash should incorporate the normalized type structure (including type arguments / compound type members).

body: `TypeDefKind.Object` is a case class, so `defKind == TypeDefKind.Object` compares against the companion object and will always be false. Use `isInstanceOf[TypeDefKind.Object]` / pattern matching so `isObject` works.

body: Unsafe type cast: casting `Right[SchemaError, Json]` to `Either[JsonError, Json]` via `asInstanceOf`. While this works at runtime because Right is covariant and the actual Right value only contains Json (not SchemaError), this bypasses the type system safety. A safer approach would be `case Right(j) => Right(j)` which creates a new Right with the correct type, though this has a small allocation cost.

`JsonBinaryCodec.bigDecimalCodec.decode(v)` is used only for validation but its `Either` result is ignored. Since `readRawValAsBytes()` uses `skipNumber` (which doesn't validate numeric syntax), invalid JSON numbers like `01` or `1e` could be accepted and stored as `Json.Number`. Handle the `Left` case (e.g., fail decoding via `in.decodeError(...)` / throw) before constructing `Number` (and ideally add a regression test).
```suggestion
        JsonBinaryCodec.bigDecimalCodec.decode(v) match {
          case Right(_) =>
            new Number(new java.lang.String(v, 0, v.length))
          case Left(error) =>
            throw error
        }
```

body: Performance issue: The sequence encoding iterates over the collection twice - once to count the size and once to encode the elements. This is inefficient for large collections. Consider using a more efficient approach such as encoding to a temporary buffer first, or using a collection method that provides the size efficiently (e.g., `.size` for collections that support it in O(1)).

This error handling pattern is confusing. The code calls `decodeError` which throws an exception, then immediately catches it in the outer try-catch block. This creates unnecessary control flow complexity. The try-catch around the `decodeError` call on line 1095 will never catch anything because `decodeError` always throws. Consider simplifying this to just call `decodeError(new DynamicOptic.Node.Field("wrapped"), new Exception(s"Expected ${wrapper.typeName.name}"))` directly.
```suggestion
                      decodeError(
                        new DynamicOptic.Node.Field("wrapped"),
                        new Exception(s"Expected ${wrapper.typeName.name}")
                      )
```

author: Copilot
body: Potential security issue: The string codec does not validate string length before unpacking, unlike BigInt and BigDecimal which check against `maxBinarySize`. A malicious payload could send an extremely large string that could cause an Out-Of-Memory error. Consider adding a size check similar to the one used for binary data (lines 198-200) to enforce the `maxBinarySize` limit on strings as well.

author: Copilot
body:
Security issue: The BigInt decoding in `decodePrimitiveValue` (lines 1215-1219) does not validate the binary length before allocating the byte array, unlike the standalone `bigIntCodec` which checks against `maxBinarySize` (lines 198-200). This inconsistency could allow a DoS attack through DynamicValue encoding. Apply the same size check here.
```suggestion
                val len = unpacker.unpackBinaryHeader()
                if (len > maxBinarySize) {
                  decodeError(s"binary size $len exceeds maximum of $maxBinarySize")
                }
```

author: Copilot
body:
The BsonSchemaCodec.scala file is in `package zio.blocks.schema.bson` and uses bson-specific annotations like `bsonField`, `bsonHint`, `bsonDiscriminator`, `bsonExclude`, and `bsonNoExtraFields`. These annotations are defined in Modifier.scala under `package bson` which makes their full path `zio.blocks.schema.bson.*`.

Since BsonSchemaCodec.scala is already in the same package (`zio.blocks.schema.bson`), it should be able to access these annotations directly without an explicit import. However, for clarity and to avoid confusion, it would be better to add an explicit import statement at the top of the file to make the dependencies clear to readers.

author: Copilot
body: The BigInt test only covers positive values. Given the critical bug in DynamicValue encoding/decoding for negative BigInt values (lines 1283 and 1326-1329 in MessagePackFormat.scala), this test should also include negative BigInt values to catch this issue. Add a test case like `roundTrip(BigInt("-" + "9" * 20))`.

author: Copilot
body:
The primitive codec implementations for BigInt use binary encoding with sign preservation via BigInteger's toByteArray/constructor. However, in the DynamicValue encoder (line 1283), BigInt is decoded using `new java.math.BigInteger(1, bs)` with a hardcoded sign of 1 (positive). This creates an inconsistency where negative BigInt values encoded as DynamicValue will be decoded as positive values, causing data corruption. The decoder should use the same constructor as the primitive codec: `new java.math.BigInteger(bs)` without the sign parameter.
```suggestion
              DynamicValue.Primitive(PrimitiveValue.BigInt(BigInt(new java.math.BigInteger(bs))))
```

author: Copilot
body: In the DynamicValue encoder for BigInt (lines 1326-1329), the sign bit is not explicitly preserved since `toByteArray` is used without the sign parameter from BigInteger. However, the corresponding decoder in `decodeDynamicValue` (line 1283) uses `new BigInteger(1, bs)` which forces a positive sign. This asymmetry means that negative BigInt values will be incorrectly decoded. The encoder should match the primitive BigInt codec implementation which correctly preserves sign.

author: Copilot
body: The volatile field `underlying` in RecursiveRecordCodec is accessed without synchronization in the encode method (line 1188). While the check `if (c eq null)` is present, there's a race condition between the null check and the actual use of `c` in the encodeUnsafe call. If `underlying` becomes null between the check and use (though unlikely with volatile), this could cause a NullPointerException. Consider using a local variable to ensure the same reference is used for both the null check and the method call, similar to the decode method.

author: Copilot
body: The DynamicValue decoder does not validate collection sizes for arrays and maps (lines 1285, 1295). A malicious input could specify extremely large sizes leading to OutOfMemoryError or denial-of-service. Similar validation should be added here as used in the sequence and map decoders (checking against MessagePackBinaryCodec.maxCollectionSize).

author: Copilot
Copilot
body: The BigInt and BigDecimal codecs do not validate binary header lengths before allocating byte arrays (lines 289-290, 307-308). A malicious input could specify extremely large lengths causing OutOfMemoryError. Add validation similar to collection size limits to prevent denial-of-service attacks.

author: Copilot
body:
The maximum collection size is set to Integer.MAX_VALUE - 8, which is approximately 2.1 billion elements. This could allow extremely large allocations that could lead to OutOfMemoryError. Consider adding a more reasonable maximum size (e.g., 10 million elements) or making this configurable to prevent potential DoS attacks through malicious input that specifies huge collection sizes.
```suggestion
  /** Maximum collection size supported to prevent memory issues.
    * This is intentionally set well below Integer.MAX_VALUE to avoid
    * attempting to allocate extremely large collections that could
    * cause OutOfMemoryError or denial-of-service conditions.
    */
  val maxCollectionSize: Int = 10_000_000
```

author: Copilot
body:
The ThreadLocal recursiveRecordCache is created but never explicitly removed except in the instanceOverrides method. If this codec is used in a thread pool (common in server applications), the ThreadLocal values will persist for the lifetime of the thread, potentially causing memory leaks. Consider documenting that users should call instanceOverrides after derivation is complete, or add a method to explicitly clean up thread-local state.
```suggestion

        /**
         * Clears the thread-local recursive record cache for the current thread.
         *
         * This should be called after codec derivation is complete when using
         * this format in long-lived thread pools, to avoid retaining per-thread
         * state indefinitely.
         */
        def clearRecursiveRecordCache(): Unit =
          recursiveRecordCache.remove()
```

author: Copilot
body: There are no tests for DynamicValue encoding/decoding despite the PR description claiming "Full support for all DynamicValue variants". The DynamicValue codec implementation has a critical bug with BigInt sign handling (lines 1283 and 1326-1329), and comprehensive tests for all DynamicValue variants (Primitive, Sequence, Record, Variant, Map) should be added to ensure correctness.

body: String concatenation using + operator creates multiple intermediate String objects. For example, "0." + ("0" * -pointPos) + digits creates at least 3 String objects. The original StringBuilder approach was more efficient. This increases allocations contrary to the PR's optimization goals.

body: The s.substring(i).getBytes(UTF_8) call creates two allocations (substring String and byte array). For strings with even a single non-ASCII character, this allocates for the entire remaining string. The original tail-recursive implementation encoded UTF-8 bytes directly to the buffer without intermediate allocations. This can significantly increase allocations, contradicting the PR's optimization goals.

author: Copilot
body:
String concatenation using + operator creates intermediate String objects. The original StringBuilder approach was more efficient for building this string.
```suggestion
        val zeros = -pointPos
        val sb    = new StringBuilder(2 + zeros + digits.length) // "0." + zeros + digits
        sb.append("0.")
        var i = 0
        while (i < zeros) {
          sb.append('0')
          i += 1
        }
        sb.append(digits)
        sb.toString
      } else if (pointPos >= digits.length) {
        val zeros = pointPos - digits.length
        val sb    = new StringBuilder(digits.length + zeros)
        sb.append(digits)
        var i = 0
        while (i < zeros) {
          sb.append('0')
          i += 1
        }
        sb.toString
      } else {
        val sb = new StringBuilder(digits.length + 1) // digits + '.'
        sb.append(digits, 0, pointPos)
        sb.append('.')
        sb.append(digits, pointPos, digits.length)
        sb.toString
```

body:
The removal of `.trim` from the token extraction changes the behavior. Previously, when processing inline context tokens, the result would be trimmed (e.g., `trimmed.substring(0, delimIdx).trim`), but now it returns the substring without trimming leading/trailing whitespace. This could cause issues if tokens have surrounding whitespace in inline contexts.
```suggestion
        val rawToken =
          if (delimIdx >= 0) currentLine.substring(linePos, delimIdx)
          else currentLine.substring(linePos)
        rawToken.trim
```

body: When an escape sequence is not recognized in non-strict mode, the original code would append the backslash and increment by 1, allowing the next iteration to append the following character. The new code doesn't append anything in this case and just continues, which means unrecognized escape sequences (when not in strict mode) are silently dropped instead of being preserved.


body:
The split behavior has changed. The original code used `split("\n", -1)` which preserves trailing empty strings, while `split('\n')` discards them. This means if the input content ends with newline characters, those empty trailing lines will be lost, potentially affecting parsing logic that depends on the exact number of lines.
```suggestion
    lines = content.split("\n", -1)
```

The condition should be `bracketStart > linePos` to correctly check if there's a key before the bracket.

It should be `currentLine.length` instead of `currentLine.length - linePos` because `afterBracket` is an absolute index into `currentLine`, not a relative offset from `linePos`. This bug causes incorrect bounds checking when verifying the presence of the colon after the array header.

author: Copilot
body:
The BigInteger constructor with signature `(int signum, byte[] magnitude)` is used here, but when decoding binary data to BigInt, the data might represent a negative number. Using signum=1 always creates a positive BigInt, which may lose the sign information. Consider using the constructor that takes just the byte array `new BigInteger(bs)` to preserve the correct sign.
```suggestion
              DynamicValue.Primitive(PrimitiveValue.BigInt(BigInt(new java.math.BigInteger(bs))))
```

author: Copilot
body:
The decodeDynamicValue function assumes that all map keys are strings (line 1300), which may not be true for all MessagePack data. If the map contains non-string keys, this will throw an exception. Consider handling non-string keys more gracefully, either by converting them to strings or by using DynamicValue.Map instead of DynamicValue.Record.
```suggestion
              val size            = unpacker.unpackMapHeader()
              val recordBuilder   = Vector.newBuilder[(String, DynamicValue)]
              val mapBuilder      = Vector.newBuilder[(DynamicValue, DynamicValue)]
              recordBuilder.sizeHint(size)
              mapBuilder.sizeHint(size)
              var idx             = 0
              var allStringKeys   = true
              while (idx < size) {
                val keyDv = decodeDynamicValue(unpacker)
                val value = decodeDynamicValue(unpacker)
                mapBuilder += ((keyDv, value))
                keyDv match {
                  case DynamicValue.Primitive(PrimitiveValue.String(s)) if allStringKeys =>
                    recordBuilder += ((s, value))
                  case _ =>
                    allStringKeys = false
                }
                idx += 1
              }
              if (allStringKeys) DynamicValue.Record(recordBuilder.result())
              else DynamicValue.Map(mapBuilder.result())
```

author: Copilot
body: The heuristic for distinguishing variants from single-field records based on uppercase first letter is fragile. A single-field case class with a capitalized field name (e.g., `Name` or `ID`) would be incorrectly decoded as a variant. Consider documenting this limitation or adding a more robust mechanism to differentiate between variants and records.

author: Copilot
body: The number decoding heuristic always prefers Int over Long, which may cause schema mismatch errors when decoding into types like `Long` or `BigInt`. If the schema expects a `Long`, but the number fits in an `Int`, this will create a `PrimitiveValue.Int` instead, potentially causing `fromDynamicValue` to fail. Consider if there's a way to use type information from the schema during decoding, or document this limitation.

This means the scala matrix dimension won't actually test different Scala versions for the Native platform. Change to: `run: sbt ++${{ matrix.scala }} test${{ matrix.platform }}`

```suggestion
        run: sbt ++${{ matrix.scala }} test${{ matrix.platform }}
```

author: jdegoes
body:
These tests are wholly insufficient. 

Every test should be EXACT MATCH with the encoded string so we can verify not only does the resulting text string "contain" the right answer, but that the whole string is, in fact, identically equal to the single right answer.

In addition, since many gains from TOON come from arrays, you should liberally use arrays of complex data and arrays of primitives in your test data types: these are notably absent of any arrays, making it impossible to use them to exercise derivation for TOON. I would expect another 10 data types, all featuring arrays in nested positions (maybe one top level), using composite and simple types.

There is probably an Apache Licensed suite of TOON structures that we should be able to parse (in another library, e.g. toon4s); you could borrow from that with proper attribution.

We should change it like so:

```scala
case class AtMapKeys(keys: Seq[DynamicValue]) extends Node
```

Methods like `DynamicOptic.atKey` can take `implicit schema: Schema[K]` and use that schema to convert the value of type `K` into a `DynamicValue`.

When this update is complete, you can delete `PatchPath` and use `DynamicOptic`, as intended.

author: Copilot
body:
The deconstructor is being called with baseOffset=0, but then the encoding loop reads from registers starting at `offset` (which was initialized to `top` from the push() call at line 1629). This mismatch means the deconstructor writes field values starting at register offset 0, while the encoding loop tries to read them starting at offset `top`. The baseOffset parameter should be `offset` instead of `0` to ensure the deconstructor writes values where the encoding loop expects to read them.
```suggestion
                deconstructor.deconstruct(regs, offset, x)
```

The phrase "years that extend 4 digits" is grammatically incorrect. It should be "years that exceed 4 digits" since the code is checking for years greater than 9999 (i.e., years with more than 4 digits).

```suggestion
      s"+$s" // '+' is required for years that exceed 4 digits, see ISO 8601:2004 sections 3.4.2, 4.1.2.4
```

author: Copilot
body:
The renamed type parameters "Upper" and "Lower" are semantically confusing given the variance annotations. With the bound "A >: Lower <: Upper", it appears that Lower is the lower bound and Upper is the upper bound. However, given the variance annotations (-Upper is contravariant, +Lower is covariant), this naming is misleading.

In the type hierarchy with these variance annotations:
- The contravariant parameter (-Upper) should represent the more general/wider type
- The covariant parameter (+Lower) should represent the more specific/narrower type
- The bound "A >: Lower <: Upper" means Lower ≤ A ≤ Upper

The original "Min" and "Max" naming was clearer in this context, as it suggested Min is the minimum (more general) bound and Max is the maximum (more specific) bound, which aligns better with the variance semantics. Consider reverting to the original parameter names or choosing names that better reflect the variance relationship, such as "Wider"/"Narrower" or "General"/"Specific".

author: Copilot
body:
The pattern match is missing a case for PrimitiveType.String. According to the test file, NString (a newtype wrapper around String) is being tested, but there's no corresponding case in this match expression. While stringCodec uses the default objectType, the pattern match should still include a case for String to be complete and handle all primitive types consistently. Add a case for `_: PrimitiveType.String` that maps to `JsonBinaryCodec.objectType`.
```suggestion
          case _: PrimitiveType.Long    => JsonBinaryCodec.longType
          case _: PrimitiveType.String  => JsonBinaryCodec.objectType
```

author: Copilot
body:
The pattern match should explicitly handle all PrimitiveType cases for completeness and consistency with the pattern used elsewhere in the codebase (e.g., lines 395-424). While PrimitiveType.String, BigInt, BigDecimal, and java.time types correctly fall through to the default case that returns objectType, explicitly listing them would make the code more maintainable and self-documenting. Consider adding explicit cases for these types that map to JsonBinaryCodec.objectType.
```suggestion
          case _: PrimitiveType.Boolean        => JsonBinaryCodec.booleanType
          case _: PrimitiveType.Byte           => JsonBinaryCodec.byteType
          case _: PrimitiveType.Char           => JsonBinaryCodec.charType
          case _: PrimitiveType.Short          => JsonBinaryCodec.shortType
          case _: PrimitiveType.Float          => JsonBinaryCodec.floatType
          case _: PrimitiveType.Int            => JsonBinaryCodec.intType
          case _: PrimitiveType.Double         => JsonBinaryCodec.doubleType
          case _: PrimitiveType.Long           => JsonBinaryCodec.longType
          case _: PrimitiveType.Unit.type      => JsonBinaryCodec.unitType
```
