author: Copilot
body:
The pattern match should explicitly handle all PrimitiveType cases for completeness and consistency with the pattern used elsewhere in the codebase (e.g., lines 642-671). While PrimitiveType.String, BigInt, BigDecimal, and java.time types correctly fall through to the default case that returns objectType, explicitly listing them would make the code more maintainable and self-documenting. Consider adding explicit cases for these types that map to AvroBinaryCodec.objectType.
```suggestion
                case _: PrimitiveType.Boolean        => AvroBinaryCodec.booleanType
                case _: PrimitiveType.Byte           => AvroBinaryCodec.byteType
                case _: PrimitiveType.Char           => AvroBinaryCodec.charType
                case _: PrimitiveType.Short          => AvroBinaryCodec.shortType
                case _: PrimitiveType.Float          => AvroBinaryCodec.floatType
                case _: PrimitiveType.Int            => AvroBinaryCodec.intType
                case _: PrimitiveType.Double         => AvroBinaryCodec.doubleType
                case _: PrimitiveType.Long           => AvroBinaryCodec.longType
                case _: PrimitiveType.Unit.type      => AvroBinaryCodec.unitType
                case _: PrimitiveType.String         => AvroBinaryCodec.objectType
                case _: PrimitiveType.BigInt         => AvroBinaryCodec.objectType
                case _: PrimitiveType.BigDecimal     => AvroBinaryCodec.objectType
                case _: PrimitiveType.Instant        => AvroBinaryCodec.objectType
                case _: PrimitiveType.LocalDate      => AvroBinaryCodec.objectType
                case _: PrimitiveType.LocalTime      => AvroBinaryCodec.objectType
                case _: PrimitiveType.LocalDateTime  => AvroBinaryCodec.objectType
                case _: PrimitiveType.OffsetDateTime => AvroBinaryCodec.objectType
                case _                               => AvroBinaryCodec.objectType
```

author: Copilot
body:
The mask calculation is incorrect for the case when idx >= 64. The mask is calculated as `1L << idx` before the if/else branches, but when idx >= 64, the mask should be `1L << (idx - 64)` to properly work with missing2. This will cause incorrect duplicate detection and bit manipulation for fields with index 64 and above.

The old code correctly calculated the mask within each branch, using the appropriate shift value for each case.

Done. I have marked the containing object `DerivedOpticsMacros` as `private[schema]`, which effectively hides these methods from the public API and ScalaDocs.

**Evidence:**
- Scala 2: https://github.com/natinew77-creator/zio-blocks/blob/f8d1ec12eceb2f44a5fe9c4bed6f6cccc6b4c1e5/schema/shared/src/main/scala-2/zio/blocks/schema/DerivedOptics.scala#L190

**Note:** I kept the method itself public because the Scala 2 compiler throws a `macro implementation must be public` error if the method is `private[schema]`. I tested this and received:
[error] schema/.../DerivedOptics.scala:47:75: macro implementation must be public [error] def optics(implicit schema: Schema[S]): Any = macro DerivedOpticsMacros.opticsImpl[S]


Hiding the enclosing object achieves the requested encapsulation while keeping the compiler happy.

author: vigoo
body:
Both this sbt plugin, and the mill one seem to do a lot of work to wrap `golem-cli` as commands and build steps, so sbt or mill is the outer build tool driven the packaging and deployment of a golem app. We should not do this, the CLI is not intended to be used like this - Golem's app manifest itself contains build steps and language specific profiles for these, and the Scala integration would be a new golem-cli template invoking sbt (or mill) under the hood to compile the Scala code to a single final JS file (just like the TS template is using rollup to do that).

There are many features in golem-cli which all assumes that it is the "driver" and not the other way around.

author: vigoo
body:
I don't understand what the build plugins are doing. They seem to analyse the compiled byte code to have their own concept of exported agents and then generate TypeScript code and embed that generated TypeScript code together with the Scala.js output into a TypeScript Golem app? 

This is absolutely not how it should work. The TypeScript SDK was just provided as an example of what to do but in Scala. The compilation should result in a single JS file that have the proper exports (as shown in the hand-written example https://github.com/vigoo/js-counter-agent/blob/master/main.js) - and these exports (invocation, agent metadata etc. should be implemented by the SDK based on the Scala macros that somehow register the annotated classes as agents.

body:
Thanks for patiently addressing most of the comments. Really appreciate it.

However, with this PR comment, I was personally looking forward to a solution. I kind of get your explanation but not fully.

My point was when an agent trait extends BaseAgent, "registry/definition/binding" is essentially ensuring they implement these functionalities in `BaseAgent`. Regardless of the language, I would wish `BaseAgent` has everything other than the actual agent-methods, an `Agent` can do.    Example: `getId`.  This is not something user write in their `agent` trait, but they get it. When they annotate their agent-implementation, this `getId` will now have an implementation. otherwise, it won't compile. Now you can see the maintainaibility of this SDK that we developers can easily extend things in BaseAgent and work backwards to satisfy the compiler (when it comes to Rust and Scala, and to some extent TS too)

For example: It was easy for @vigoo  to add `load_snapshot` and `save_snapshot` (like [here](https://github.com/golemcloud/golem/blob/main/sdks/ts/packages/golem-ts-sdk/src/baseAgent.ts#L98)) into the `BaseAgent`and work backwards (atleast for the most part, and then he changed things in macros). I am not saying that's how everyone should do, but it will be really easy for both maintainers and users and see what's going on. In this case, users see they are extending `BaseAgent` but, they don't see anything in it.

author: Copilot
body:
The `FireAndForget` branch always fails the returned `Future` by throwing, even when the method’s declared return type is `Unit`. If the intent is “call returns `Future[Out]`”, this should complete successfully for `Out = Unit` (or be prevented at compile time). Consider returning `runFireAndForget(...).asInstanceOf[Future[Out]]` (assuming `Out` is `Unit` for all fire-and-forget methods), or changing the API so `call` is only available for `Awaitable` and `trigger/schedule` are the supported entrypoints for fire-and-forget methods.
```suggestion
          runFireAndForget(method, input).asInstanceOf[Future[Out]]
```

author: Copilot
body:
This decoder attempts to support both wrapped (`tag == "unstructured-text"|"unstructured-binary"`) and unwrapped (`tag == "inline"|"url"`) shapes, but the “unwrapped” path is currently incorrect: it still reads `payload.selectDynamic("val")`, even though `payload` is already the inner value for unwrapped shapes. A safer approach is to branch on `tag`: if wrapped, pass `payload.selectDynamic("val")`; otherwise pass `payload` directly to `decodeTextValue` / `decodeBinaryValue`.
```suggestion
        val innerPayload =
          if (tag == "unstructured-text") payload.selectDynamic("val")
          else payload
        decodeTextValue(innerTag, innerPayload).map(ElementValue.UnstructuredText.apply)
      case ElementSchema.UnstructuredBinary(_) =>
        val payload  = value.selectDynamic("val")
        val innerTag =
          if (tag == "unstructured-binary") payload.selectDynamic("tag").asInstanceOf[String]
          else tag
        val innerPayload =
          if (tag == "unstructured-binary") payload.selectDynamic("val")
          else payload
        decodeBinaryValue(innerTag, innerPayload).map(ElementValue.UnstructuredBinary.apply)
```

author: Copilot
body:
Converting `prim-float64` to `Int`/`Long` via `toInt`/`toLong` will silently truncate fractional values (and may overflow without an error). If the host ever sends non-integral JS numbers, this will round toward zero and corrupt data. Consider rejecting non-whole numbers (e.g., check `raw.isWhole`) and validating bounds before converting.
```suggestion
          if (!raw.isValidInt)
            Left(s"Value $raw is out of Int range for IntType")
          else if (!raw.isWhole())
            Left(s"Non-integral numeric value $raw cannot be decoded as IntType")
          else
            Right(IntValue(raw.toInt))
        case (LongType, "prim-s64") =>
          val raw = node.selectDynamic("val").asInstanceOf[Double]
          if (!raw.isValidLong)
            Left(s"Value $raw is out of Long range for LongType (prim-s64)")
          else if (!raw.isWhole())
            Left(s"Non-integral numeric value $raw cannot be decoded as LongType (prim-s64)")
          else
            Right(LongValue(raw.toLong))
        case (LongType, "prim-float64") =>
          val raw = node.selectDynamic("val").asInstanceOf[Double]
          if (!raw.isValidLong)
            Left(s"Value $raw is out of Long range for LongType (prim-float64)")
          else if (!raw.isWhole())
            Left(s"Non-integral numeric value $raw cannot be decoded as LongType (prim-float64)")
          else
            Right(LongValue(raw.toLong))
```

author: Copilot
body: The “flags parsing + cloud/local router reachability check + build + deploy + repl + output grep” flow is duplicated across multiple scripts in `golem/examples/*.sh`. This makes it easy for behavior to drift (e.g., timeouts, error patterns, router port guidance). Consider extracting shared logic into a single `examples/lib.sh` (or similar) and sourcing it from each script, keeping only the script-specific `script_file` and expected output checks.

author: Copilot
body: There is significant code duplication between the if branch (lines 1417-1441) and the else branch (lines 1444-1464). Both branches iterate through fields and populate the same map and req1/req2 variables using identical logic. This duplication could be eliminated by extracting the common logic after the if-else block, since both branches need to perform this initialization.

author: Copilot
body: In the else branch where cached `infos` exist, the `offset` variable is not updated and remains at its initial value of 0. This will cause `usedRegisters` (line 1629) to be incorrect when using cached field infos for recursive types. The offset should be calculated from the cached `infos` array by summing up each field's codec.valueOffset, similar to what's done in lines 1438-1439 of the if branch.

author: jdegoes
body:
ZIO Schema also supports "newtypes", which are seen in Scala 3 as opaque types, and should be tested. 

For example:

```scala
opaque type Age = Int
object Age {
  def apply(i: Int): Age = i 
}
```

The derived schema will be a `Wrapper` (Reflect.Wrapper), and should result in a `Lens[Age, Int]` (I think; actually would be better as `Iso[Age, Int]`, but we don't have `Iso` yet in optics).

author: Copilot
body: The discriminate method uses a catch-all pattern with asInstanceOf without an explicit pattern match for CaseNodeInfo. While this should work correctly given that CaseInfo only has two implementations (CaseLeafInfo and CaseNodeInfo), it would be more type-safe and maintainable to use an explicit pattern match. Consider replacing the catch-all case with a specific pattern for CaseNodeInfo to make the code more explicit and fail faster if the sealed hierarchy is extended in the future.

author: Copilot
body:
The discriminate method uses a catch-all pattern with asInstanceOf without an explicit pattern match for EnumNodeInfo. While this should work correctly given that EnumInfo only has two implementations (EnumLeafInfo and EnumNodeInfo), it would be more type-safe and maintainable to use an explicit pattern match. Consider replacing the catch-all case with a specific pattern for EnumNodeInfo to make the code more explicit and fail faster if the sealed hierarchy is extended in the future.
```suggestion
    case eli: EnumLeafInfo    => eli
    case eni: EnumNodeInfo[A] => eni.discriminate(x)
```

body: With `getOrElse` you are silently swallowing errors, best to preserve the errors and reflect those in the return type. I'd add a more powerful variant, `applyOrFail`, which returns `Either[SchemaError, S]`, and then this one can just "leave it unchanged" if there is an error. That way you can get errors if you want, but you don't have to see them if you don't care.

author: jdegoes
body:
Not only should you delete the old Pair stuff, but you should have comprehensive constructors for all possible typed patches, which use the implicit schema to do serialization to `DyanmicValue` (which is what `DynamicPatch`) requires in many cases.

So `Patch.*` constructors will be universal and type safe and, when necessary, accept both the raw user values (e.g. `S`, `A` ) and the schemas necessary to convert them into dynamic values.

author: Copilot
body: The optimization for small enumerations (with 8 or fewer cases) has been removed. Previously, small enumerations used a linear search through the enumValueInfos array which can be faster for very small enums due to better cache locality and no hash computation overhead. Now all enumerations use the hash map lookup regardless of size. Consider keeping the optimization for small enumerations while still supporting aliases, by adding alias checks to the linear search path.

author: Copilot
body: The comparison operator was inverted from `<=` to `>`, but the logic branches were not swapped accordingly. In the original code, when the condition `halfUlp + even <= dotOne` was true, it would execute the complex calculation. Now with `> 0`, when the condition is true (meaning halfUlp + even > dotOne), it executes a simple multiplication instead. This appears to be a logic error - the branches should have been swapped when inverting the comparison.

author: Copilot
body: The expressions `hi64 * 10L` at lines 2288 and 2303 do not account for the shift that was present in the original code. The original code computed `m10 = hi64 >>> 6` first, then `m10 = (m10 << 3) + (m10 << 1)` which equals `(hi64 >>> 6) * 10`. The new code uses `hi64 * 10L` which is shifting by a different amount. This should be `(hi64 >>> 6) * 10L` or the full expression `(hi64 << 3) + (hi64 << 1)` to maintain correctness.

author: Copilot
body: The expression `hi64 * 10L` does not account for the shift that was present in the original code. The original code computed `m10 = hi64 >>> 6` first, then `m10 = (m10 << 3) + (m10 << 1)` which equals `(hi64 >>> 6) * 10`. The new code uses `hi64 * 10L` which is shifting by a different amount. This should be `(hi64 >>> 6) * 10L` or the full expression `(hi64 << 3) + (hi64 << 1)` to maintain correctness.

author: Copilot
body: The test change updates f68's default value from `Nil` to `List(1, 2, 3)`, which is important for testing non-empty collection defaults. However, the existing test cases at lines 1537-1549 don't explicitly verify that when f68 is omitted from the JSON input, it correctly decodes to `List(1, 2, 3)`. Consider adding a test case that decodes JSON without f68 and asserts that the resulting object has f68 set to `List(1, 2, 3)` rather than `Nil`. This would provide explicit coverage for the primary bug fix in this PR.

The documentation states that mark operations "cannot be nested," but the updated implementation now supports nested marks by returning and accepting the previous mark position. The documentation should be updated to reflect that nested marks are now supported.
```suggestion
   * `resetMark()` or `rollbackToMark()` calls. Mark operations can be nested
   * by saving and restoring previous mark positions returned by `setMark()`.
   *
   * @return
   *   the position of the previous mark, or -1 if there was no previous mark
```

author: Copilot
body:
The documentation states that mark operations "cannot be nested," but the updated implementation now supports nested marks by returning and accepting the previous mark position. The documentation should be updated to reflect that nested marks are now supported.
```suggestion
   * `resetMark()` or `rollbackToMark()` calls. Mark operations can be nested:
   * each call to `setMark()` returns the previous mark position, which can be
   * restored by passing it to `resetMark()` or `rollbackToMark()`.
   *
   * @return
   *   the position of the previous mark, or -1 if no previous mark was set
```

author: Copilot
body:
The default value constructor is being invoked repeatedly during encoding for each field comparison. For a field with 9 fields like Record6, this could result in up to 9 constructor calls per encode operation.

Consider caching the default value once per field instead of calling `field.defaultValueConstructor.get.apply()` multiple times. This would improve performance and ensure correctness if the constructor has side effects.

author: Copilot
body:
[nitpick] Using `!=` for floating-point comparisons (Float and Double) may lead to unexpected behavior due to floating-point precision issues. Values that are semantically equal might not compare as equal due to rounding errors.

Consider using an epsilon-based comparison for floating-point types, or document that exact equality is intentional for this use case.

author: Copilot
body:
The `transientDefaultValue` flag is not being checked in the encoding logic. This means fields with default values will always be omitted when they equal the default value, regardless of the `transientDefaultValue` configuration setting.

Following the pattern used for optional fields (line 1237: `!transientNone || (value ne None)`) and collections (line 1243: `!transientEmptyCollection || value.asInstanceOf[Iterable[?]].nonEmpty`), each value comparison should be wrapped with a check like `!transientDefaultValue || (value != field.defaultValueConstructor.get.apply())`.

This will break the test at line 1711-1714 which expects `withTransientDefaultValue(false)` to force encoding of all fields including those with default values.

author: Copilot
body:
The default value constructor is called inside the encoding loop for every field that has a default value (line 1291). This means the default value is reconstructed every time an object is encoded, which could impact performance for frequently encoded objects.

Consider caching the default value in the FieldInfo during initialization, rather than storing a constructor function. This would eliminate the repeated function calls during encoding.

2. Pre-computing indices of optional/collection fields during initialization

This would avoid checking all fields when only a subset need default values set.


author: Copilot
body:
The condition at line 1293 allows variants in the enumeration, but line 1302 sets the constructor to `null` for variant cases. This creates a potential `NullPointerException` when decoding at lines 583 and 612, where `constructor.construct(null, 0)` is called without null-checking the constructor.

Since intermediate variant types (sealed traits/abstract classes) should not be directly instantiated, they should be excluded from the enumeration optimization. The condition at line 1293 should be:
```scala
if (variant.cases.forall(_.value.asRecord.exists(_.fields.isEmpty))) {
```
instead of:
```scala
if (variant.cases.forall(case_ => case_.value.asRecord.exists(_.fields.isEmpty) || case_.value.isVariant)) {
```

This would ensure the enumeration optimization is only used for true enumerations (case objects with no fields), not for ADTs with intermediate variants.

author: Copilot
body:
`copy` resets `rejectExtraFields` to `false` by default, which silently disables the strict decoding you may have enabled with `withRejectExtraFields(true)` when chaining other `with*` methods (e.g., `withFieldNameMapper`). An attacker could exploit this by adding unexpected fields to JSON objects that will be accepted instead of rejected, bypassing intended strict schema enforcement. Fix by preserving the current flag in `copy`, e.g., change to:

```scala
private def copy(
  fieldNameMapper: NameMapper = fieldNameMapper,
  caseNameMapper: NameMapper = caseNameMapper,
  rejectExtraFields: Boolean = this.rejectExtraFields
) =
```

```suggestion
    rejectExtraFields: Boolean = this.rejectExtraFields
```

author: Copilot
body:
The method `rename` and val `rename` have the same name, which creates ambiguity and reduces code readability. Within the method body, `rename` in `Modifier.config(rename, name)` should resolve to the val (since it's used without parentheses), but this naming collision makes the code harder to understand and maintain. Consider renaming either the method (e.g., `def apply(name: String)`) or the val (e.g., `val renameKey = "json.rename"`) to improve clarity.
```suggestion
  def rename(name: String): Modifier.config = Modifier.config(renameKey, name)

  private[json] val renameKey = "json.rename"
```

The `compute` method will receive `null` for the first invocation when the key doesn't exist, but the lambda parameter `n: Int` is a non-nullable primitive. This will cause a NullPointerException. Use `computeIfAbsent` to initialize, or handle null explicitly by using `Integer` instead of `Int`, e.g., `(_: (String, String), n: Integer) => if (n == null) 0 else n + 1`.


body:
The logic for tracking record counters is incorrect. `putIfAbsent` returns the previous value associated with the key, or `null` if there was no mapping. When the key doesn't exist, it inserts 0 and returns `null`, which will cause a NullPointerException when used in `number + 1`. The correct implementation should use `getOrDefault` or handle the null case. For example: `val number = Option(recordCounters.get().putIfAbsent(fqn, 0)).getOrElse(0)` or use `compute`/`merge` methods instead.
```suggestion
          val number = recordCounters.get().getOrDefault(fqn, 0)
```

body:
The custom encoder violates Avro's array encoding format. According to the Avro specification, arrays are encoded as a series of blocks where each block is preceded by a count. The encoding must be terminated with a zero-length block. Add `e.writeInt(0)` after encoding all elements (line 397) to properly terminate the array.
```suggestion
                }
                e.writeInt(0)
```

body:
This custom implementation should include a docstring explaining why it replaces `java.io.ByteArrayOutputStream` (performance optimization, avoiding unnecessary allocations) and documenting the buffer growth strategy.
```suggestion

/**
 * Custom implementation replacing `java.io.ByteArrayOutputStream`.
 *
 * This class is used for performance optimization and to avoid unnecessary allocations
 * that can occur with the standard `ByteArrayOutputStream`. The buffer growth strategy
 * doubles the buffer size when more space is needed, or grows to fit the required length
 * if doubling is insufficient. This minimizes the number of allocations and copies,
 * especially for large or unpredictable output sizes.
 */
```

author: Copilot
body:
The new implementation significantly changes the logic for detecting generic tuples. Consider adding a comment explaining why both conditions are necessary and what edge cases this handles compared to the previous `*:` type equality check.
```suggestion

    /**
     * Determines if the given type is a generic tuple (i.e., a tuple type constructed via `*:`),
     * as opposed to a standard tuple class (Tuple2, Tuple3, etc.).
     * 
     * The check `tpe <:< TypeRepr.of[Tuple]` ensures the type is a subtype of Tuple,
     * while `!defn.isTupleClass(tpe.typeSymbol)` excludes standard tuple classes.
     * 
     * This is necessary because in Scala 3, generic tuples (e.g., `Int *: String *: EmptyTuple`)
     * are represented differently from standard tuple classes, and the previous `*:` type equality
     * check did not distinguish these cases. This logic ensures that only true generic tuples
     * (recursively defined via `*:`) are matched, handling edge cases where a type might be a
     * standard tuple class or a generic tuple type.
     */
```

The function doesn't handle potential null values from `qual.termSymbol`. If `qual` doesn't have a term symbol, `qual.termSymbol` could be null, leading to a NullPointerException when calling `.typeRef`.
```suggestion
      case TypeRef(qual, "Type") =>
        val termSym = qual.termSymbol
        if (termSym != null)
          termSym.typeRef.baseClasses.exists(_.fullName == "zio.prelude.Newtype")
        else
          false
      case _ => false
```

Similar to the previous function, this doesn't handle potential null values from `qual.termSymbol`. Additionally, if no matching base class is found, it returns the original `tpe`, but this might indicate an error condition that should be handled more explicitly.
```suggestion
        val termSym = qual.termSymbol
        if (termSym == null) fail(s"qual.termSymbol was null for $qual in zioPreludeNewtypeDealias")
        val companionTpe = termSym.typeRef
        companionTpe.baseClasses
          .map(companionTpe.baseType)
          .collectFirst {
            case AppliedType(atTpe, List(typeArg)) if atTpe.typeSymbol.fullName == "zio.prelude.Newtype" =>
              typeArg.dealias
          }
          .getOrElse(fail(s"No matching zio.prelude.Newtype base class found for $tpe"))
      case _ =>
        fail(s"zioPreludeNewtypeDealias called with non-TypeRef: $tpe")
```

body:
Using hard-coded indices to access variant cases may lead to brittle tests if the order changes. Consider retrieving cases by name or using an explicit mapping to ensure stability in the test.
```suggestion
        val record1 = variant.flatMap(_.cases.find(_.name == "Case-1").flatMap(_.value.asRecord))
        val record2 = variant.flatMap(_.cases.find(_.name == "Case-2").flatMap(_.value.asRecord))
        val record3 = variant.flatMap(_.cases.find(_.name == "Case-3").flatMap(_.value.asRecord))
```

author: Copilot
body:
Relying on the order of cases from variant reflection can be fragile. Consider asserting on the presence of expected case names without strictly enforcing their order.
```suggestion
        assert(variant.map(_.cases.map(_.name)))(isSome(hasSameElements(Seq("Case-1", "Case-2", "Case-3")))) &&
```

author: Copilot
body:
Using a hard-coded order for variant cases might lead to fragile tests if the internal order changes; consider verifying the set of case names in a way that is independent of order.
```suggestion
        assert(variant.map(_.cases.map(_.name).toSet))(isSome(equalTo(Set("MissingValue", "NullValue", "Value")))) &&
```

author: Copilot
body:
Using 'return' inside a fold lambda can be non-idiomatic in Scala. It is recommended to refactor this logic to avoid non-local returns for improved clarity and maintainability.
```suggestion
      acc.flatMap { s =>
        single match {
          case LensPair(optic, LensOp.Replace(a))           => new Right(optic.replace(s, a))
          case PrismPair(optic, PrismOp.Replace(a))         => optic.replaceOrFail(s, a)
          case OptionalPair(optic, OptionalOp.Replace(a))   => optic.replaceOrFail(s, a)
          case TraversalPair(optic, TraversalOp.Replace(a)) => optic.modifyOrFail(s, _ => a)
        }
```

Instead of increasing the registers array size by one, consider using an exponential growth strategy (e.g. doubling the capacity) to reduce frequent reallocations and improve performance.
```suggestion
      val newSize = Math.max(2 * registers.length, 1)
      registers = java.util.Arrays.copyOf(registers, newSize)
```

body: Resetting the registers array to only one element after a GC collection may lead to inefficient allocations if multiple registers are expected; consider initializing with a larger default capacity.

author: jdegoes
body:
Is the overhead of `WeakReference` really worth the memory saving?

If object registers are 'zeroed out', we can collect the things they reference, without collecting the registers themselves, which might be a nice midway point.

A is defined on 2 places here in `apply` of `SchemaAspect` and on `examples` method because it takes values of `A`. Compiler complains about 

```
type mismatch;
 found   : value.type (with underlying type A)
 required: A
 ```
 
if I move A to `trait SchemaAspect` level, than other aspects like identity would need a type parameter which would hurt user experience e.g. `@@ identity[Person]`

author: sviezypan

author: jdegoes
body: `examples` should use the type parameter because it can only apply to a single node (the node where the optic points to). In fact, you should not be able to write `examples` in a type-safe way without specifying `Min = A` and `Max = A`.

author: jdegoes
body:
Rather than casting to `Reflect.Bound`, which would cause class cast exception if the `F` type parameter is not `Binding`, you can instead require evidence that `F` is binding. I used to have an `IsBinding` type class in `schema.binding` package, I suggest re-adding that. It should have a method to convert a `Reflect[F, A]` into `Reflect[Binding, A]` for any `A`. 

Then you can delete the cast here and also in the below method and make the code type safe.

author: jdegoes
body:
```suggestion
  def aspect[B, Min >: B, Max <: B](part: Optic[A, B], aspect: SchemaAspect[Min, Max]): Reflect.Bound[A] =
```

This is more precise and will allow "recursive" schema aspects to be applied starting from a certain location.

author: jdegoes
body: See also comment about `IsBinding` type class.

author: jdegoes
body: Btw instead of `IsBinding` you could generalize `SchemaAspect` to use `F[_, _]` for the binding type parameter, and then specific aspects could only be defined for `Binding`. In some ways that might be cleaner.

author: jdegoes
body:
The other method I would **love** to have here is `def recursive(implicit ev1: Min >:> Any, ev2: Max <:< Nothing): SchemaAspect[Min, Max]`.

By default this would return the same thing, but subclasses could override to provide recursive behavior.

For example, the `doc` aspect could override to provide a version that attaches the docs to all subtrees, not just the top one (note: doc does not make sense to have a recursive version, so I would not actually do that, but something like `kebabCase` makes sense to have in both flat and recursive variants, so you can do `foo @@ kebabCase` but also `foo @@ kebabCase.recursive`).
